"Then as to unjust hurts -- and gains also, supposing the injustice to bring gain -- of these we may heal as many as are capable of being healed, regarding them as diseases of the soul; and the cure of injustice will take the following direction."

"What direction?"
refashioned:
"No one should assume that all harms are the same as injuries, or that injuries come in simply two kinds -- voluntary and involuntary. Involuntary harms are just as numerous and serious as voluntary ones.

"Consider carefully whether what I am about to say is right or wrong: I maintain, Cleinias and Megillus, that someone who harms another involuntarily does not commit an involuntary injury. I would not legislate for such an act as though it were an involuntary injury. Rather, I would say that an involuntary hurt, whether large or small, is not an injury at all. Conversely, when a benefit is conferred in a wrongful way, the person providing the benefit may actually be committing an injury.

"My fundamental principle is this: the mere act of giving something to or taking something from another person is neither just nor unjust in itself. What the legislator must examine is whether people do good or harm to each other from a just intention and principle. The law must focus on the distinction between injustice and mere harm. Where there is harm, the law should restore what was damaged, rescue what was ruined, raise up what has fallen, and make whole what was wounded or destroyed. After compensating for injury, the law should always work to transform the enmity between the parties into friendship."

"Very good."

"As for unjust harms -- and unjust gains too, when injustice produces profit -- we must heal as many of these as we can, treating them as diseases of the soul. The cure of injustice will take the following form."

"What form?"
elegant: "Not all harms are injuries. An involuntary harm is not an injury at all; a wrongly conferred benefit may be one. What matters is not the act of giving or taking but the intention and principle behind it. The legislator must distinguish harm from injustice: for harm, the law restores and compensates; after compensation, it converts enmity into friendship. For injustice -- a disease of the soul -- the law must heal."
summary: The Stranger draws his crucial distinction: harm and injustice are different things. Involuntary harm is not injury. What makes an act just or unjust is the intention behind it, not the act itself. The law has two tasks: compensate for harms (restoring what was damaged, converting enmity to friendship) and cure injustice (treating it as a disease of the soul).
distilled: Harm and injustice are distinct: involuntary harm is not injury at all, and the law must both compensate for harms and heal injustice as a disease of the soul.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is arguably the philosophical heart of Book 9. The distinction between 'harm' (blabe) and 'injustice' (adikia) resolves the dilemma posed in 9.18-9.20. Harm is a factual matter (did damage occur?) while injustice concerns the soul's condition (was the soul disordered by passion, pleasure, or ignorance?). This allows the Stranger to maintain that all injustice is involuntary while still legislating differently for different harms. The law's dual function -- compensating harm and curing injustice -- directly applies the Gorgias' medical analogy (477a-479e): just as medicine heals the body, punishment heals the soul. The remarkable principle that 'the mere giving or taking away of anything is not to be described either as just or unjust' relocates justice entirely in the agent's intention and principle -- anticipating Kant's later emphasis on the moral will. The goal of converting 'enmity to friendship' through law anticipates restorative justice theories by millennia. Compare also Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics 5.8, which develops a related but distinct account of voluntary and involuntary injustice.

narrative:
"When any one commits any injustice, small or great, the law will admonish and compel him either never at all to do the like again, or never voluntarily, or at any rate in a far less degree; and he must in addition pay for the hurt. Whether the end is to be attained by word or action, with pleasure or pain, by giving or taking away privileges, by means of fines or gifts, or in whatsoever way the law shall proceed to make a man hate injustice, and love or not hate the nature of the just -- this is quite the noblest work of law.

"But if the legislator sees any one who is incurable, for him he will appoint a law and a penalty. He knows quite well that to such men themselves there is no profit in the continuance of their lives, and that they would do a double good to the rest of mankind if they would take their departure, inasmuch as they would be an example to other men not to offend, and they would relieve the city of bad citizens. In such cases, and in such cases only, the legislator ought to inflict death as the punishment of offences."
refashioned:
"Whenever anyone commits an injustice, whether small or great, the law shall admonish and compel him to never repeat the offense -- or at least never to do so willingly, or to do so far less often. In addition, he must compensate for any harm caused. Whether this is achieved through words or actions, through pleasure or pain, through granting or revoking privileges, through fines or rewards -- by whatever means the law can make a person hate injustice and love, or at least not despise, the nature of what is just -- this is the noblest work of law.

"But when the legislator encounters someone who is incurable, he will prescribe a specific law and penalty for that person. He understands clearly that continued life brings no benefit to such individuals, and that their death would serve the rest of humanity doubly: first, by providing a deterrent example to others, and second, by relieving the city of harmful citizens. Only in such cases -- and absolutely no others -- should the legislator impose the death penalty."
elegant: "The law's noblest work is to make offenders hate injustice and love justice -- through any means necessary: words, actions, pleasure, pain, privileges, fines, or rewards. Offenders must also compensate for the harm they caused. But for incurable criminals, continued life benefits neither them nor the city. Death serves only as deterrent example and civic relief -- and should be imposed in no other case."
summary: The Stranger defines the noblest purpose of law: to make people hate injustice and love justice, using any effective means while also requiring compensation for harm. Death is reserved exclusively for incurable offenders, where it serves as deterrence and civic cleansing.
distilled: The highest purpose of law is to make offenders love justice through education and reform; death is reserved solely for incurable criminals whose execution serves as deterrence.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage distills the Laws' entire philosophy of punishment into its clearest statement. The law has two functions: (1) reform the curable offender by instilling hatred of injustice and love of justice, and (2) remove the incurable through death. This directly applies the Gorgias' argument (525b-d) about punishment in the afterlife, where reformable souls are punished for their own benefit while incurable souls are punished purely as examples. The expansive list of methods -- 'word or action, pleasure or pain, giving or taking away privileges, fines or gifts' -- shows remarkable flexibility, anticipating modern debates about whether rehabilitation works better through positive or negative reinforcement. The phrase 'love or not hate the nature of the just' is deliberately graduated -- the law aims for love of justice but will settle for absence of hatred, acknowledging realistic psychological limits. The strict limitation of death to incurable cases ('in such cases only') represents a restraint on capital punishment unusual in ancient legislation.

narrative:
"What you have said appears to me to be very reasonable," Cleinias said, "but will you favour me by stating a little more clearly the difference between hurt and injustice, and the various complications of the voluntary and involuntary which enter into them?"

"I will endeavour to do as you wish," the Stranger replied. "Concerning the soul, thus much would be generally said and allowed, that one element in her nature is passion, which may be described either as a state or a part of her, and is hard to be striven against and contended with, and by irrational force overturns many things."

"Very true."

"And pleasure is not the same with passion, but has an opposite power, working her will by persuasion and by the force of deceit in all things."

"Quite true."
refashioned:
"What you have said seems very reasonable," Cleinias said, "but could you explain more clearly the difference between harm and injustice, and the way voluntary and involuntary elements complicate both?"

"I will try," the Stranger answered. "Regarding the soul, most people would accept that one element of its nature is passion -- whether we call it a condition or a distinct part of the soul -- which is extremely difficult to resist and which overpowers many things through sheer irrational force."

"Very true."

"Pleasure is distinct from passion, however. It operates by the opposite method, achieving its aims through persuasion and the power of deception rather than through brute force."

"Quite true."
elegant: Cleinias asks for a clearer account of harm versus injustice and how voluntariness enters in. The Stranger begins with the soul's structure: passion is an irrational force that overpowers through sheer violence, while pleasure works by the opposite means -- persuasion and deception.
summary: At Cleinias' request, the Stranger begins clarifying the harm-injustice distinction by analyzing the soul. Two forces drive wrongdoing: passion, which overpowers through brute irrational force, and pleasure, which works through persuasion and deception.
distilled: The soul contains two forces that cause wrongdoing: passion, which overpowers by irrational force, and pleasure, which works through persuasion and deception.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The analysis of the soul's motivational structure echoes Republic 4 (435b-441c), where Socrates divides the soul into rational, spirited (thumoeides), and appetitive parts. The Stranger's account here is somewhat different: he identifies passion (thumos) and pleasure (hedone) as two distinct forces rather than mapping them neatly onto the Republic's tripartite structure. The characterization of passion as operating by 'irrational force' and pleasure by 'persuasion and deceit' anticipates the distinction between crimes of violence and crimes of fraud that will structure much of Book 9's criminal taxonomy. This dual analysis also parallels the Phaedrus (246a-254e) myth of the chariot, where the spirited horse responds to force while the appetitive horse requires more subtle management. The claim that pleasure works by 'deceit' (apate) connects to the Philebus (36c-44d) analysis of false pleasures -- pleasures that misrepresent their objects.

narrative:
"A man may truly say that ignorance is a third cause of crimes. Ignorance, however, may be conveniently divided by the legislator into two sorts: there is simple ignorance, which is the source of lighter offences, and double ignorance, which is accompanied by a conceit of wisdom; and he who is under the influence of the latter fancies that he knows all about matters of which he knows nothing.

"This second kind of ignorance, when possessed of power and strength, will be held by the legislator to be the source of great and monstrous crimes, but when attended with weakness, will only result in the errors of children and old men; and these he will treat as errors, and will make laws accordingly for those who commit them, which will be the mildest and most merciful of all laws."
refashioned:
"Ignorance is truthfully identified as a third cause of crime. The legislator may usefully divide ignorance into two types: simple ignorance, which produces lighter offenses, and double ignorance, which is accompanied by the delusion that one is wise. The person suffering from double ignorance believes he understands perfectly the very matters about which he knows nothing.

"When double ignorance is combined with power and strength, the legislator will recognize it as the source of terrible and monstrous crimes. But when it accompanies weakness -- as in children and the elderly -- it produces only errors and mistakes. The legislator will treat these as mere errors and design the mildest and most merciful laws for those who commit them."
elegant: "Ignorance is the third cause of crime. Simple ignorance produces lighter offenses. Double ignorance -- the delusion of wisdom about matters one does not understand -- produces monstrous crimes when combined with power, but only harmless errors when found in the weak, such as children and the elderly. The law treats the latter with maximum mercy."
summary: The Stranger identifies ignorance as the third cause of crime alongside passion and pleasure. Simple ignorance leads to minor offenses. Double ignorance -- believing one knows what one does not -- causes either monstrous crimes (when combined with power) or harmless errors (in the weak), with the law treating the latter most mercifully.
distilled: Ignorance is the third cause of crime: simple ignorance causes minor offenses, while double ignorance (the conceit of wisdom) causes either monstrous crimes or harmless errors depending on the offender's power.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. 'Double ignorance' (diploe agnoia) is a distinctively Socratic concept, most fully developed in the Apology (21b-23b), where Socrates discovers that those reputed wise are actually doubly ignorant -- they do not know and do not know that they do not know. The Sophist (229c) explicitly defines double ignorance as 'thinking one knows when one does not.' By making this a cause of crime, the Stranger extends the epistemological analysis of the earlier dialogues into jurisprudence. The grading by power is pragmatically shrewd: the same cognitive defect that is merely foolish in a child becomes catastrophic in a ruler. This anticipates Lord Acton's observation about power corrupting, but the Platonic version is more precise -- power does not corrupt so much as amplify existing ignorance to catastrophic effect. The merciful treatment of children's and elders' errors reflects a nuanced understanding of diminished capacity that has parallels in modern criminal law.

narrative:
"You are perfectly right," Cleinias said.

"We all of us remark of one man that he is superior to pleasure and passion, and of another that he is inferior to them; and this is true."

"Certainly."

"But no one was ever yet heard to say that one of us is superior and another inferior to ignorance."

"Very true."

"We are speaking of motives which incite men to the fulfilment of their will; although an individual may be often drawn by them in opposite directions at the same time."

"Yes, often."
refashioned:
"You are perfectly right," Cleinias agreed.

"We commonly say that one person masters his pleasures and passions while another is mastered by them -- and this is accurate."

"Certainly."

"But no one has ever been heard to say that one person is 'superior to ignorance' while another is 'inferior to it.'"

"Very true."

"These are the forces that drive people to fulfill their desires, even though an individual may frequently be pulled in opposite directions by them simultaneously."

"Yes, frequently."
elegant: We say people can be stronger or weaker than their pleasures and passions. But no one speaks of being 'stronger' or 'weaker' than ignorance -- it operates differently. These forces -- passion, pleasure, and ignorance -- drive human action, often pulling the same person in contradictory directions.
summary: The Stranger observes an asymmetry among the three causes of wrongdoing: people can resist pleasure and passion (some succeed, others fail), but no one speaks of resisting ignorance in the same way. All three forces motivate action and often pull individuals in conflicting directions.
distilled: While people can be stronger or weaker than pleasure and passion, no one speaks of overcoming ignorance in the same way -- it operates as a fundamentally different kind of motivational force.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. This subtle observation reveals something important about the nature of ignorance compared to passion and pleasure. One can feel passion or desire and resist it through self-control (enkrateia/sophrosyne) -- a capacity Plato discusses extensively in Republic 4 (430e-432a) and Protagoras (352b-358d). But ignorance is not something one feels and resists; by its very nature, the ignorant person does not recognize his ignorance. This is precisely what makes double ignorance (9.24) so dangerous -- unlike passion, which announces itself through felt intensity, ignorance conceals itself behind the illusion of knowledge. The observation that these motives 'often draw a person in opposite directions at the same time' echoes the Republic's argument for the soul's division into parts (436b-441c): the same person simultaneously desires and refrains, proving the existence of multiple motivational sources within a single soul.

narrative:
"And now I can define to you clearly, and without ambiguity, what I mean by the just and unjust, according to my notion of them: When anger and fear, and pleasure and pain, and jealousies and desires, tyrannize over the soul, whether they do any harm or not -- I call all this injustice. But when the opinion of the best, in whatever part of human nature states or individuals may suppose that to dwell, has dominion in the soul and orders the life of every man, even if it be sometimes mistaken, yet what is done in accordance therewith, the principle in individuals which obeys this rule, and is best for the whole life of man, is to be called just; although the hurt done by mistake is thought by many to be involuntary injustice.

"Leaving the question of names, about which we are not going to quarrel, and having already delineated three sources of error, we may begin by recalling them somewhat more vividly to our memory: One of them was of the painful sort, which we denominate anger and fear."
refashioned:
"Now I can define justice and injustice clearly and unambiguously, as I understand them: When anger, fear, pleasure, pain, jealousy, or desire tyrannize the soul -- whether or not they produce any external harm -- I call that condition injustice. But when a person's judgment about what is best holds dominion over the soul and governs his life -- in whatever part of human nature a state or individual locates that faculty -- then what is done according to that judgment, and the disposition of the person who follows it, should be called just. This is true even when such judgment is occasionally mistaken, though most people would call the resulting harm 'involuntary injustice.'

"Setting aside terminological disputes, and having identified three sources of wrongdoing, let us recall them vividly. The first was the painful kind: anger and fear."
elegant: "Injustice is when passion, pleasure, fear, or desire tyrannize the soul -- regardless of external harm. Justice is when the soul's best judgment governs one's life -- even if that judgment occasionally errs. The three sources of error are: first, the painful emotions -- anger and fear."
summary: The Stranger defines injustice as any condition where emotions tyrannize the soul, regardless of whether external harm results. Justice is governance by the soul's best judgment, even when that judgment occasionally errs. He recaps the three sources of wrongdoing, beginning with anger and fear.
distilled: Injustice is the tyranny of emotion over the soul regardless of external harm; justice is the soul's governance by its best rational judgment, even when sometimes mistaken.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This definition of justice as internal psychic order is the Laws' version of the Republic's famous definition (443c-444a), where justice is each part of the soul performing its proper function. The key innovation here is the explicit decoupling of justice from external consequences: even harmful actions done from correct rational judgment are just, and even harmless states of emotional domination are unjust. This internalizes justice completely -- it becomes a description of the soul's condition rather than of outcomes. The phrase 'in whatever part of human nature states or individuals may suppose that to dwell' is deliberately ecumenical, avoiding commitment to the Republic's specific tripartite psychology. The 'opinion of the best' (doxa tou aristou) is a broader concept than the Republic's 'reason' (logistikon), perhaps reflecting the Laws' more modest epistemological claims. The three sources of error -- anger/fear, pleasure/desire, and ignorance -- map loosely onto but do not exactly replicate the three parts of the Republic's soul.

narrative:
"Quite right," Cleinias said.

"There was a second consisting of pleasures and desires, and a third of hopes, which aimed at true opinion about the best. The latter being subdivided into three, we now get five sources of actions; and for these five we will make laws of two kinds."

"What are the two kinds?"

"There is one kind of actions done by violence and in the light of day, and another kind of actions which are done in darkness and with secret deceit, or sometimes both with violence and deceit; the laws concerning these last ought to have a character of severity."

"Naturally."
refashioned:
"Correct," said Cleinias.

"The second source was pleasures and desires. The third was hopes and aspirations aimed at forming a true opinion about what is best. Since this third category subdivides into three, we arrive at five sources of action in total. For these five, we will create two categories of law."

"What two categories?"

"One category covers actions committed openly, through violence, in broad daylight. The other covers actions committed secretly, through cunning and deception in the shadows -- or sometimes through both violence and deception combined. Laws governing this last category should be especially severe."

"Naturally."
elegant: "The second source of error is pleasure and desire; the third is misdirected hope for the best. Subdividing the third gives five sources of action total. Laws will be of two kinds: for open violence and for secret deception. Crimes combining both deserve the severest treatment."
summary: The Stranger completes his taxonomy: five sources of action (anger/fear, pleasure/desire, and three subdivisions of misdirected hope) and two categories of law -- one for open violence, another for secret deception. Crimes combining both deserve the harshest penalties.
distilled: Five sources of wrongdoing yield two categories of criminal law: one for open violence and another for secret deception, with the combination of both meriting the greatest severity.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The distinction between crimes of violence (bia) and crimes of deception (dolos) is fundamental to Greek legal thought and recurs in Aristotle's treatment of injustice (Nicomachean Ethics 5.2, 1131a). The Stranger's judgment that deception deserves greater severity than open violence reflects a moral hierarchy common in Plato: deception corrupts the soul more deeply because it subverts rationality itself, while violence at least operates in the open. Compare Gorgias 483a-b on the distinction between persuasion and force, and Republic 382a-c on the 'true lie' that deceives the soul as the worst form of falsehood. The five-fold taxonomy (anger/fear, pleasure/desire, and three types of misdirected hope) is somewhat obscure -- the subdivision of the third category is not fully elaborated. Some scholars suggest it corresponds to hopes about bodily goods, external goods, and goods of the soul, though the text does not make this explicit.

narrative:
"And now let us return from this digression and complete the work of legislation. Laws have been already enacted by us concerning the robbers of the Gods, and concerning traitors, and also concerning those who corrupt the laws for the purpose of subverting the government.

"A man may very likely commit some of these crimes, either in a state of madness or when affected by disease, or under the influence of extreme old age, or in a fit of childish wantonness, himself no better than a child. And if this be made evident to the judges elected to try the cause, on the appeal of the criminal or his advocate, and he be judged to have been in this state when he committed the offence, he shall simply pay for the hurt which he may have done to another; but he shall be exempt from other penalties, unless he have slain some one, and have on his hands the stain of blood.

"And in that case he shall go to another land and country, and there dwell for a year; and if he return before the expiration of the time which the law appoints, or even set his foot at all on his native land, he shall be bound by the guardians of the law in the public prison for two years, and then go free."
refashioned:
"Let us now return from this philosophical digression to the practical work of legislation. We have already enacted laws concerning temple robbery, treason, and the violent subversion of the state's legal order.

"It is quite possible that someone may commit one of these crimes while in a state of madness, affected by illness, under the debilitating influence of extreme old age, or in a fit of childish recklessness no different from actual childhood. If this is demonstrated to the satisfaction of the judges trying the case -- on the appeal of the defendant or his advocate -- and the judges determine that the offender was in such a condition when he committed the crime, he shall simply compensate for any harm done to another person, but shall be exempt from all further punishment.

"The one exception is if he has killed someone and bears the stain of blood. In that case, he must leave the country and live abroad for one year. If he returns before the legally appointed time has expired, or so much as sets foot on his native soil, the guardians of the law shall imprison him for two years, after which he shall go free."
elegant: "Returning to legislation: we have covered temple robbery, treason, and subversion of law. If any of these crimes were committed in madness, illness, extreme old age, or childish recklessness, and the judges are satisfied of this condition, the offender pays only for the harm done and is otherwise exempt -- unless he killed someone, in which case he must live abroad for a year. Early return means two years in prison."
summary: The Stranger provides for diminished capacity: offenders who committed serious crimes while mad, ill, extremely old, or childishly reckless are liable only for compensating the harm they caused. The exception is homicide, which requires one year of exile. Returning early triggers two years of imprisonment.
distilled: Crimes committed under madness, illness, extreme age, or childish recklessness are punished only by compensation for harm caused, except homicide, which requires a year of exile.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage introduces a remarkably developed concept of diminished capacity into ancient criminal law. The four categories -- madness, disease, extreme old age, and childish recklessness -- cover most of what modern law would recognize as conditions mitigating criminal responsibility. The principle directly applies the harm-injustice distinction developed in 9.21: since the offender's soul was not in a condition to be 'unjust' in the full sense (his rational faculty was impaired), the law addresses only the harm caused. The exception for homicide -- requiring exile even under diminished capacity -- reflects the deep Greek belief in blood pollution (miasma), which was considered automatic and independent of the killer's mental state. Compare Oresteia (Eumenides 448-452) on the pollution that attaches to homicide regardless of justification. The requirement of purification for blood guilt operates on a religious-ritual plane separate from the criminal law's concern with justice and reform.

narrative:
"Having begun to speak of homicide, let us endeavour to lay down laws concerning every different kind of homicides, and, first of all, concerning violent and involuntary homicides.

"If any one in an athletic contest, and at the public games, involuntarily kills a friend, and he dies either at the time or afterwards of the blows which he has received; or if the like misfortune happens to any one in war, or military exercises, or mimic contests of which the magistrates enjoin the practice, whether with or without arms, when he has been purified according to the law brought from Delphi relating to these matters, he shall be innocent.

"And so in the case of physicians: if their patient dies against their will, they shall be held guiltless by the law.

"And if one slay another with his own hand, but unintentionally, whether he be unarmed or have some instrument or dart in his hand; or if he kill him by administering food or drink or by the application of fire or cold, or by suffocating him, whether he do the deed by his own hand, or by the agency of others, he shall be deemed the agent, and shall suffer one of the following penalties: If he kill the slave of another in the belief that he is his own, he shall bear the master of the dead man harmless from loss, or shall pay a penalty of twice the value of the dead man, which the judges shall assess; but purifications must be used greater and more numerous than for those who committed homicide at the games -- what they are to be, the interpreters whom the God appoints shall be authorized to declare."
refashioned:
"Since we have begun discussing homicide, let us try to establish laws for every type of killing, starting with violent and involuntary homicides.

"If someone accidentally kills a companion during an athletic competition or public games -- whether death occurs immediately or later from injuries sustained -- or if a similar accident occurs during warfare, military training, or the practice combat exercises mandated by magistrates, whether armed or unarmed: after undergoing purification according to the Delphic law governing such matters, the killer shall be declared innocent.

"The same applies to physicians: if a patient dies contrary to the doctor's intention, the law shall hold the doctor guiltless.

"If someone kills another by his own hand but without intent -- whether unarmed or holding a weapon or projectile, whether through administering food or drink, applying heat or cold, or suffocation, whether acting personally or through others -- that person is legally the responsible agent and shall face the following penalties: If he kills another person's slave believing the slave to be his own, he must either compensate the dead man's owner fully for the loss or pay double the assessed value of the slave. Purification rites must be performed -- more extensive than those required for deaths in athletic contests. The divinely appointed interpreters shall determine the specific rituals required."
elegant: "Starting with involuntary homicide: deaths in athletic contests, warfare, military training, or mandated combat exercises are innocent after Delphic purification. Physicians whose patients die unintentionally are guiltless. Other unintentional killings -- by any means, direct or indirect -- make the killer legally responsible. Killing another's slave by mistake requires compensation at double value. Purification rites, more extensive than for contest deaths, are prescribed by divine interpreters."
summary: The Stranger begins homicide law with involuntary killing. Deaths in sanctioned contests, war, and military training are innocent after purification. Physicians are guiltless for patient deaths. Other unintentional killings require penalties and extensive purification rites determined by religious authorities.
distilled: Involuntary homicides in sanctioned activities (contests, war, medicine) are innocent after purification; other unintentional killings require compensation and more extensive religious purification.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage maps closely onto historical Athenian homicide law. The distinction between killings in sanctioned activities and other involuntary homicides corresponds to established Greek legal categories. Athenian law explicitly exempted deaths in athletic competitions from prosecution (though purification was still required). The Delphic oracle's role as the authoritative source for purification law was a real feature of Greek religious practice -- the oracle at Delphi was the supreme authority on matters of ritual pollution and purification. The physician's exemption from liability for patient death anticipates modern medical malpractice distinctions between negligence and unavoidable outcomes. The detailed enumeration of killing methods -- weapons, food, drink, fire, cold, suffocation, personal or through agents -- shows the legislator's need to cover all possible scenarios, a concern shared by actual Greek legal codes. The assessment of slaves by monetary value reflects the harsh realities of Greek slavery, though the requirement of purification even for slave deaths acknowledges a level of moral seriousness to all killing.

narrative:
"And if a man kills his own slave, when he has been purified according to law he shall be quit of the homicide. And if a man kills a freeman unintentionally, he shall undergo the same purification as he did who killed the slave.

"But let him not forget also a tale of olden time, which is to this effect: He who has suffered a violent end, when newly dead, if he has had the soul of a freeman in life, is angry with the author of his death; and being himself full of fear and panic by reason of his violent end, when he sees his murderer walking about in his own accustomed haunts, he is stricken with terror and becomes disordered, and this disorder of his, aided by the guilty recollection, is communicated by him with overwhelming force to the murderer and his deeds.

"Wherefore also the murderer must go out of the way of his victim for the entire period of a year, and not himself be found in any spot which was familiar to him throughout the country. And if the dead man be a stranger, the homicide shall be kept from the country of the stranger during a like period.

"If any one voluntarily obeys this law, the next of kin to the deceased, seeing all that has happened, shall take pity on him, and make peace with him, and show him all gentleness. But if any one is disobedient, either ventures to go to any of the temples and sacrifice unpurified, or will not continue in exile during the appointed time, the next of kin to the deceased shall proceed against him for murder; and if he be convicted, every part of his punishment shall be doubled."
refashioned:
"If a man kills his own slave, he shall be free of the homicide charge once purified according to law. If he unintentionally kills a free person, the same purification applies.

"But he should also remember an ancient belief: a person who has died violently, if he possessed a free man's soul in life, is angry at his killer in the immediate aftermath of death. Filled with fear and panic from his violent end, the newly dead spirit -- upon seeing his killer walking freely in familiar places -- is seized with terror and becomes disordered. This spiritual disturbance, reinforced by the killer's own guilty conscience, transfers itself to the murderer with overwhelming force and corrupts his actions.

"For this reason, the killer must stay completely away from his victim's former haunts for a full year and must not be found anywhere the victim was known to frequent. If the dead person was a foreigner, the killer must similarly avoid the foreigner's home country for the same period.

"If the killer obeys this law willingly, the victim's next of kin -- observing his compliance and good faith -- should take pity on him, make peace with him, and treat him with gentleness. But if he disobeys -- whether by entering temples to sacrifice while still unpurified or by returning from exile before the appointed time -- the next of kin shall prosecute him for murder. If convicted, every element of his punishment shall be doubled."
elegant: "Killing one's own slave requires only purification. Unintentional killing of a free person requires the same purification plus one year of exile from the victim's haunts. Ancient tradition holds that a violently killed freeman's spirit is angry and disturbed, and this disorder communicates itself to the killer. Willing compliance with exile earns the victim's family's forgiveness and gentleness. Disobedience -- entering temples unpurified or breaking exile -- results in murder prosecution with doubled punishment."
summary: The Stranger prescribes purification for killing one's own slave and one year of exile for unintentionally killing a free person. An ancient tradition about the vengeful spirits of the violently killed justifies the exile requirement. Willing compliance earns reconciliation; disobedience doubles the punishment.
distilled: Unintentional killing of a free person requires purification plus one year of exile from the victim's haunts; compliance earns forgiveness from the victim's family, while disobedience doubles all penalties.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'tale of olden time' about vengeful spirits of the violently dead reflects genuine Greek religious beliefs about blood pollution and ghostly retribution. The concept of miasma (ritual pollution) from homicide was deeply embedded in Greek culture -- Aeschylus' Eumenides dramatizes the Furies pursuing Orestes for matricide. The Stranger integrates this traditional belief into rational legislation: the exile serves both a ritual purpose (separation from pollution) and a psychological one (the killer's guilty conscience, combined with the community's horror, creates a toxic situation that only distance can resolve). The goal of converting the victim's family from enmity to pity and peace through the killer's willing compliance anticipates the restorative justice principles stated in 9.21. The doubling of punishment for disobedience creates a strong incentive for compliance and respects the religious dimension of homicide that operates alongside its legal dimension. Compare the Euthyphro (4b-c), where Euthyphro's father is accused of allowing a laborer to die through neglect -- a case that would fall under these provisions.

narrative:
"And if the next of kin do not proceed against the perpetrator of the crime, then the pollution shall be deemed to fall upon his own head -- the murdered man will fix the guilt upon his kinsman, and he who has a mind to proceed against him may compel him to be absent from his country during five years, according to law.

"If a stranger unintentionally kill a stranger who is dwelling in the city, he who likes shall prosecute the cause according to the same rules. If he be a metic, let him be absent for a year, or if he be an entire stranger, in addition to the purification, whether he have slain a stranger, or a metic, or a citizen, he shall be banished for life from the country which is in possession of our laws. And if he return contrary to law, let the guardians of the law punish him with death; and let them hand over his property, if he have any, to him who is next of kin to the sufferer.

"And if he be wrecked, and driven on the coast against his will, he shall take up his abode on the seashore, wetting his feet in the sea, and watching for an opportunity of sailing; but if he be brought by land, and is not his own master, let the magistrate whom he first comes across in the city release him and send him unharmed over the border."
refashioned:
"If the next of kin fails to prosecute the killer, the pollution transfers to the kinsman himself -- the spirit of the murdered person will direct his wrath at the negligent relative. Anyone may then bring charges against the kinsman, compelling him to leave the country for five years under law.

"If a foreigner unintentionally kills another foreigner residing in the city, anyone may prosecute under the same rules. A resident alien shall be exiled for one year. A complete foreigner, regardless of whether he killed a foreigner, a resident alien, or a citizen, shall undergo purification and be permanently banished from the country. If he returns illegally, the guardians of the law shall execute him and transfer whatever property he owns to the victim's next of kin.

"If the exile is shipwrecked and driven onto our coast involuntarily, he shall stay on the seashore with his feet in the water, watching for the first opportunity to sail away. If he arrives overland and not of his own will, the first magistrate he encounters shall release him and escort him safely across the border."
elegant: "If the next of kin fails to prosecute, the pollution falls on him instead, and anyone may compel his five-year exile. For foreigners who kill unintentionally: resident aliens are exiled one year; complete foreigners are banished for life after purification. Illegal return means death. But involuntary arrivals -- shipwrecked on the coast or brought overland against their will -- shall be protected and sent safely out of the country."
summary: The Stranger addresses negligent prosecution and the status of foreign killers. If kin fail to prosecute, they absorb the pollution. Resident aliens who kill unintentionally face one year of exile; complete foreigners face permanent banishment. Involuntary return due to shipwreck or force is handled humanely.
distilled: Negligent kin who fail to prosecute absorb the murder pollution; foreign killers face exile graduated by residency status; and involuntary returns by shipwreck are treated humanely.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The transfer of pollution from killer to negligent kin is a powerful legal mechanism that ensures prosecution occurs. It reflects the Greek belief that blood pollution (miasma) is objective and must be addressed -- it does not simply dissipate but attaches to whoever is responsible for failing to purify the community. The graduated penalties by status (metic vs. complete stranger) reflect the different degrees of obligation and protection in the Greek legal system: metics (metoikoi) were long-term resident aliens with defined legal rights, while complete strangers (xenoi) had fewer protections. The remarkably humane provision for shipwrecked exiles -- allowing them to stay on the beach 'wetting his feet in the sea' while waiting for a ship -- suggests a legal fiction: as long as the exile is technically in the water and actively seeking departure, he has not truly 'returned.' This combination of strict principle with compassionate exception exemplifies the Laws' aspiration to be 'wise and loving parents' rather than rigid tyrants (9.14).

narrative:
"If any one slays a freeman with his own hand and the deed be done in passion, in the case of such actions we must begin by making a distinction. For a deed is done from passion either when men suddenly, and without intention to kill, cause the death of another by blows and the like on a momentary impulse, and are sorry for the deed immediately afterwards; or again, when after having been insulted in deed or word, men pursue revenge, and kill a person intentionally, and are not sorry for the act.

"And, therefore, we must assume that these homicides are of two kinds, both of them arising from passion, which may be justly said to be in a mean between the voluntary and involuntary; at the same time, they are neither of them anything more than a likeness or shadow of either. He who treasures up his anger, and avenges himself, not immediately and at the moment, but with insidious design, and after an interval, is like the voluntary; but he who does not treasure up his anger, and takes vengeance on the instant, and without malice prepense, approaches to the involuntary; and yet even he is not altogether involuntary, but only the image or shadow of the involuntary.

"Wherefore about homicides committed in hot blood, there is a difficulty in determining whether in legislating we shall reckon them as voluntary or as partly involuntary. The best and truest view is to regard them respectively as likenesses only of the voluntary and involuntary, and to distinguish them accordingly as they are done with or without premeditation. And we should make the penalties heavier for those who commit homicide with angry premeditation, and lighter for those who do not premeditate, but smite upon the instant; for that which is like a greater evil should be punished more severely, and that which is like a less evil should be punished less severely: this shall be the rule of our laws."

"Certainly," Cleinias agreed.
refashioned:
"When someone kills a free person with his own hands in a moment of passion, we must draw an important distinction. There are two kinds of passionate killing. In the first, a person suddenly and without any intention to kill strikes another on impulse and is immediately sorry. In the second, a person who has been insulted deliberately pursues revenge, kills intentionally, and feels no remorse.

"Both types arise from passion and occupy a middle ground between the fully voluntary and the fully involuntary. Neither is truly one or the other -- each is merely a likeness or shadow of its category. The person who nurses his anger and takes revenge after deliberation and delay resembles the voluntary killer. The person who strikes instantly without premeditation resembles the involuntary one. Yet even he is not truly involuntary -- merely an image of it.

"This makes passionate homicide genuinely difficult to classify. The most accurate approach is to treat each type as merely resembling voluntary or involuntary homicide and to distinguish them by the presence or absence of premeditation. Premeditated passionate homicide deserves heavier punishment; unpremeditated passionate homicide deserves lighter. What resembles a greater evil should be punished more severely, and what resembles a lesser evil, less severely. This shall be our law's guiding principle."

"Certainly," Cleinias agreed.
elegant:
"Passionate homicides fall between voluntary and involuntary -- shadows of both. Two types: the person who strikes suddenly on impulse and immediately regrets it resembles the involuntary killer; the person who nurses anger and plans revenge resembles the voluntary. Neither is truly either. The law should treat premeditated passionate killing more severely and unpremeditated more leniently, matching punishment to the degree of evil each resembles."

"Certainly," said Cleinias.
summary: The Stranger analyzes homicide in passion as occupying a middle ground between voluntary and involuntary. Premeditated revenge killing resembles voluntary homicide; spontaneous striking resembles involuntary. Neither is fully either -- they are shadows. Penalties should be heavier for premeditated passion and lighter for spontaneous action.
distilled: Passionate homicides are shadows of both voluntary and involuntary killing, and the law should punish premeditated passionate killing more severely than spontaneous passionate killing.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The metaphor of 'likenesses' and 'shadows' (eikonas, skias) of the voluntary and involuntary is philosophically rich, recalling the Republic's ontology of images and originals (509d-511e, the Divided Line). Just as sensible objects are imperfect images of the Forms, passionate homicides are imperfect images of the pure categories of voluntary and involuntary action. This passage also introduces a remarkably nuanced category that anticipates the modern legal concept of 'crimes of passion' and the distinction between first-degree (premeditated) and second-degree (spontaneous) murder. Historical Athenian law did recognize killings in anger as a distinct category: the court at the Palladion handled involuntary homicide, the Areopagus handled voluntary, and the court at Delphinion handled justified killing. The Stranger's intermediate category adds philosophical precision to these established distinctions. The criterion of remorse (sorry vs. not sorry) as an indicator of degree of voluntariness is psychologically astute.

narrative:
"Let us proceed: If any one slays a free man with his own hand, and the deed be done in a moment of anger, and without premeditation, let the offender suffer in other respects as the involuntary homicide would have suffered, and also undergo an exile of two years, that he may learn to school his passions.

"But he who slays another from passion, yet with premeditation, shall in other respects suffer as the former; and to this shall be added an exile of three instead of two years -- his punishment is to be longer because his passion is greater.

"The manner of their return shall be on this wise (and here the law has difficulty in determining exactly; for in some cases the murderer who is judged by the law to be the worse may really be the less cruel, and he who is judged the less cruel may be really the worse, and may have executed the murder in a more savage manner, whereas the other may have been gentler. But in general the degrees of guilt will be such as we have described them. Of all these things the guardians of the law must take cognisance):

"When a homicide of either kind has completed his term of exile, the guardians shall send twelve judges to the borders of the land; these during the interval shall have informed themselves of the actions of the criminals, and they shall judge respecting their pardon and reception; and the homicides shall abide by their judgment."
refashioned:
"Continuing: If someone kills a free person in a spontaneous moment of anger, without premeditation, the offender shall suffer the same consequences as an involuntary killer and shall additionally be exiled for two years, so that he may learn to control his passions.

"If someone kills from passion but with premeditation, the same consequences apply, but the exile extends to three years instead of two -- the longer punishment reflecting the greater indulgence of anger.

"The process of return shall work as follows (though the law acknowledges a genuine difficulty here: sometimes the offender who is legally classified as worse may actually have been less cruel in his actions, while the one classified as less culpable may have killed more savagely. The legal categories will generally correspond to actual degrees of guilt, but not always. The guardians of the law must remain alert to these discrepancies):

"When a killer of either type has completed his exile, the guardians shall send twelve judges to the country's borders. These judges shall have spent the intervening period investigating the exile's conduct and behavior, and they shall decide whether to grant pardon and allow his return. The exiled killers must accept the judges' decision."
elegant: "Unpremeditated passionate killing: exile for two years to learn self-control. Premeditated passionate killing: exile for three years. The law admits its categories may not perfectly match reality -- some 'worse' offenders may be gentler in practice -- and the guardians must stay alert to this. After exile, twelve judges investigate the exile's conduct abroad and decide whether to grant return."
summary: The Stranger prescribes two years of exile for unpremeditated passionate killing and three for premeditated. He acknowledges that legal categories may not perfectly correspond to actual guilt. After exile, twelve judges evaluate the exile's behavior during his absence before deciding on his readmission.
distilled: Unpremeditated passionate killing merits two years of exile and premeditated merits three, with twelve judges evaluating the exile's conduct before allowing return.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The explicit acknowledgment that legal categories may not match reality is a remarkably self-aware moment in ancient jurisprudence. The Stranger recognizes what modern legal theorists call the problem of 'fit' -- the gap between abstract legal classifications and the complexity of individual cases. This epistemic humility is characteristic of the Laws' mature approach to legislation. The purpose of exile -- 'that he may learn to school his passions' -- directly applies the rehabilitative theory of punishment from 9.22. The twelve-judge panel sent to the borders represents a sophisticated parole mechanism: judges who have actively investigated the exile's conduct during his absence, rather than simply counting time served. This anticipates modern parole boards that evaluate rehabilitation rather than merely tracking sentence completion. The differential exile periods (two vs. three years) reflect the principle from 9.32 that premeditated passion resembles voluntary crime more closely and therefore deserves greater punishment.

narrative:
"But if after they have returned home, any one of them in a moment of anger repeats the deed, let him be an exile, and return no more; or if he returns, let him suffer as the stranger was to suffer in a similar case.

"He who kills his own slave shall undergo a purification, but if he kills the slave of another in anger, he shall pay twice the amount of the loss to his owner.

"And if any homicide is disobedient to the law, and without purification pollutes the agora, or the games, or the temples, he who pleases may bring to trial the next of kin to the dead man for permitting him, and the murderer with him, and may compel the one to exact and the other to suffer a double amount of fines and purifications; and the accuser shall himself receive the fine in accordance with the law.

"If a slave in a fit of passion kills his master, the kindred of the deceased man may do with the murderer -- provided only they do not spare his life -- whatever they please, and they will be pure; or if he kills a freeman who is not his master, the owner shall give up the slave to the relatives of the deceased, and they shall be under an obligation to put him to death, but this may be done in any manner which they please."
refashioned:
"If any returned exile repeats the offense in another fit of anger, he shall be permanently banished with no possibility of return. Should he return anyway, he shall suffer the same penalty a foreigner would face in the same situation.

"Killing one's own slave requires only purification. Killing another person's slave in anger requires paying the owner double the slave's value.

"If any killer disobeys the law by appearing unpurified in the marketplace, at the games, or in the temples, any citizen may bring charges against both the next of kin (for failing to enforce the law) and the murderer himself. The court may compel double fines and purifications from both parties, and the person who brought the charge shall receive the fine as prescribed by law.

"If a slave kills his master in a fit of passion, the dead man's relatives may do anything they wish with the killer -- the only restriction being that they may not spare his life. They will be ritually pure after carrying out the punishment. If a slave kills a free person who is not his master, the slave's owner must surrender him to the victim's relatives, who are then obligated to execute the slave by whatever means they choose."
elegant: "Repeat passionate killers are permanently exiled. Killing one's own slave requires only purification; killing another's slave costs double the value. Unpurified killers who pollute public spaces bring liability on both themselves and their kin. A slave who kills his master must die at the hands of the master's family -- they may choose the method but must not spare him. A slave who kills another free person is surrendered by his owner to the victim's family for execution."
summary: The Stranger covers several specific cases: repeat offenders are permanently exiled; killing one's own slave requires only purification while killing another's costs double value; unpurified killers who enter public spaces bring charges on both themselves and negligent kin; slaves who kill free persons must be executed.
distilled: Repeat passionate killers face permanent exile; killings of slaves require purification or double compensation; and slaves who kill free persons must be executed by the victim's family.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The permanent exile for repeat offenders reflects a practical acknowledgment that rehabilitation sometimes fails -- connecting to the curable/incurable distinction established in 9.5 and 9.22. The provision allowing anyone to prosecute negligent kin for tolerating an unpurified killer in public spaces creates a public enforcement mechanism that does not depend solely on private parties. This anticipates the Athenian legal institution of the graphe (public prosecution), which allowed any citizen to bring certain charges on behalf of the community. The treatment of slave killings is harsh by modern standards but represents the legal reality of ancient Greek slavery. Notably, even the master who kills his own slave must undergo purification -- acknowledging the moral gravity of all killing. The requirement that the master's family must not spare the slave who killed his master ensures that personal relationships between slave and family do not subvert the deterrent function of punishment.

narrative:
"And if -- which is a rare occurrence, but does sometimes happen -- a father or a mother in a moment of passion slays a son or daughter by blows, or some other violence, the slayer shall undergo the same purification as in other cases, and be exiled during three years; but when the exile returns the wife shall separate from the husband, and the husband from the wife, and they shall never afterwards beget children together, or live under the same roof, or partake of the same sacred rites with those whom they have deprived of a child or of a brother.

"And he who is impious and disobedient in such a case shall be brought to trial for impiety by any one who pleases.

"If in a fit of anger a husband kills his wedded wife, or the wife her husband, the slayer shall undergo the same purification, and the term of exile shall be three years. And when he who has committed any such crime returns, let him have no communication in sacred rites with his children, neither let him sit at the same table with them, and the father or son who disobeys shall be liable to be brought to trial for impiety by any one who pleases.

"If a brother or a sister in a fit of passion kills a brother or a sister, they shall undergo purification and exile, as was the case with parents who killed their offspring: they shall not come under the same roof, or share in the sacred rites of those whom they have deprived of their brethren, or of their children."
refashioned:
"If -- rarely but not impossibly -- a parent kills a son or daughter in a moment of passionate rage, through blows or other violence, the killer shall undergo the standard purification and be exiled for three years. Upon returning, the husband and wife must permanently separate: they shall never again have children together, live under the same roof, or participate in the same religious ceremonies with those they have deprived of a child or sibling. Anyone who violates these requirements may be prosecuted for impiety by any citizen.

"If a husband kills his wife in anger, or a wife her husband, the same purification applies and the exile lasts three years. Upon return, the killer shall have no part in sacred rites with their children and shall not share their table. Any parent or child who violates this may be prosecuted for impiety.

"If a brother or sister kills a sibling in a fit of passion, the same purification and exile apply. The killer shall not live under the same roof as, or share sacred rites with, those they have deprived of family members."
elegant: "A parent who kills a child in passion: purification and three years' exile, then permanent separation from their spouse -- no more children, no shared home, no shared rites. A spouse who kills the other: purification, three years' exile, then exclusion from all sacred communion with the children. A sibling who kills a sibling: same purification, exile, and exclusion from shared household and rites. Violation of these restrictions is prosecutable as impiety."
summary: The Stranger addresses passionate killing within families: parent killing child, spouse killing spouse, sibling killing sibling. All require purification and three years of exile. Upon return, the killer faces permanent separation from remaining family members in household and religious life. Violations are prosecutable as impiety.
distilled: Family killings in passion -- parent of child, spouse of spouse, sibling of sibling -- require purification, three years of exile, and permanent exclusion from the household and sacred rites of the surviving family.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage treats domestic homicide with a characteristic combination of severity and nuance. The three-year exile matches the penalty for premeditated passionate killing (9.33), suggesting that family killing is treated as the more serious form of passionate homicide regardless of premeditation. The post-return restrictions -- permanent separation, exclusion from shared meals and rites -- function as a form of ongoing punishment and pollution-management that goes beyond simple exile and return. The requirement that parents must separate and never have children together again may reflect a belief that the household itself has been polluted by the act and cannot be restored to its former state. The enforceability through impiety prosecutions (asebeia) connects these domestic provisions to the religious framework that underpins Greek family law. Compare Euripides' Medea, which dramatizes the horror of a parent killing children, and the Oresteia, which centers on the cycle of family murder and its resolution. The provision for spousal killing is gender-neutral -- both husband killing wife and wife killing husband receive identical treatment, which is noteworthy for the period.

narrative:
"And he who is disobedient shall be justly liable to the law concerning impiety, which relates to these matters.

"If any one is so violent in his passion against his parents, that in the madness of his anger he dares to kill one of them, if the murdered person before dying freely forgives the murderer, let him undergo the purification which is assigned to those who have been guilty of involuntary homicide, and do as they do, and he shall be pure.

"But if he be not acquitted, the perpetrator of such a deed shall be amenable to many laws -- he shall be amenable to the extreme punishments for assault, and impiety, and robbing of temples, for he has robbed his parent of life; and if a man could be slain more than once, most justly would he who in a fit of passion has slain father or mother undergo many deaths.

"How can he, whom, alone of all men, even in defence of his life, and when about to suffer death at the hands of his parents, no law will allow to kill his father or his mother who are the authors of his being, and whom the legislator will command to endure any extremity rather than do this -- how can he, I say, lawfully receive any other punishment? Let death then be the appointed punishment of him who in a fit of passion slays his father or his mother."
refashioned:
"Anyone who violates these family restrictions shall rightly face prosecution under the impiety laws.

"If someone is so overcome by rage against his parents that in the madness of his anger he dares to kill one of them -- and if the dying parent, before death, freely forgives the murderer -- then the killer shall undergo the purification prescribed for involuntary homicide and shall follow those procedures. He will then be considered pure.

"But if the dying parent does not forgive, the killer is subject to multiple laws simultaneously -- the harshest penalties for assault, impiety, and temple robbery, since he has robbed his parent of the most sacred possession: life itself. If it were possible to kill a man more than once, the parricide would justly deserve multiple deaths.

"Consider: this is the one person in all of humanity whom no law will permit to kill his parents even in self-defense, even when facing death at their hands. The legislator commands him to endure any extremity rather than raise his hand against the authors of his existence. How, then, can he lawfully receive any punishment less than death? Death must be the sentence for anyone who kills a father or mother in a fit of passion."
elegant: "If a dying parent forgives their killer, the parricide undergoes involuntary homicide purification and is clean. If not, he faces the combined penalties for assault, impiety, and temple robbery -- and would deserve multiple deaths if that were possible. Since no law permits killing one's parents even in self-defense, no punishment short of death can be appropriate for parricide."
summary: The Stranger addresses parricide. If the dying parent forgives, the killer undergoes involuntary homicide purification. Without forgiveness, the parricide faces the combined weight of laws against assault, impiety, and temple robbery. Since no one may kill a parent even in self-defense, death is the only adequate punishment.
distilled: Parricide is punishable by death unless the dying parent forgives the killer, since no law permits killing one's parents even in self-defense.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The treatment of parricide as the most extreme crime reflects a near-universal moral conviction in ancient cultures. The striking provision for deathbed forgiveness by the dying parent introduces a remarkable element of victim autonomy into criminal law -- the most severe crime can be mitigated by the victim's own choice to forgive. The argument from the prohibition of self-defense against parents is logically elegant: if you may not kill your parent even to save your own life, then killing a parent under any lesser provocation must be punishable by death. The comparison to 'robbing of temples' (since the parent has been 'robbed' of life) treats life as sacred in a quasi-religious sense. The wish that the parricide 'could be slain more than once' expresses the perceived inadequacy of any finite punishment for this crime. Compare Sophocles' Oedipus at Colonus, where Oedipus argues that his parricide was involuntary and therefore not truly guilty -- exactly the kind of case the Stranger's forgiveness provision might address.

narrative:
"But if brother kills brother in a civil broil, or under other like circumstances, if the other has begun, and he only defends himself, let him be free from guilt, as he would be if he had slain an enemy; and the same rule will apply if a citizen kill a citizen, or a stranger a stranger. Or if a stranger kill a citizen or a citizen a stranger in self-defence, let him be free from guilt in like manner; and so in the case of a slave who has killed a slave.

"But if a slave have killed a freeman in self-defence, let him be subject to the same law as he who has killed a father; and let the law about the remission of penalties in the case of parricide apply equally to every other remission. Whenever any sufferer of his own accord remits the guilt of homicide to another, under the idea that his act was involuntary, let the perpetrator of the deed undergo a purification and remain in exile for a year, according to law."

"Enough has been said of murders violent and involuntary and committed in passion: we have now to speak of voluntary crimes done with injustice of every kind and with premeditation, through the influence of pleasures, and desires, and jealousies."

"Very good."
refashioned:
"If a brother kills a brother during civil unrest or similar circumstances, and the victim started the conflict while the killer acted only in self-defense, the killer shall be free from guilt -- just as if he had killed an enemy in battle. The same rule applies when a citizen kills a citizen, or a stranger kills a stranger, in self-defense. Similarly, if a stranger kills a citizen, or a citizen kills a stranger, in self-defense, both shall be guiltless. And the same holds for a slave who kills another slave.

"However, if a slave kills a free person in self-defense, he shall be subject to the same law that governs parricide. The provision for forgiveness that applies to parricide shall likewise apply to all other cases where remission is possible. Whenever a dying victim voluntarily forgives his killer, believing the act to have been involuntary, the killer must still undergo purification and remain in exile for one year as required by law."

"We have now said enough about violent, involuntary, and passionate homicides. It is time to discuss voluntary crimes committed with full injustice and premeditation, driven by pleasures, desires, and jealousies."

"Very good."
elegant: "Self-defense killing in civil conflict, between citizens, between strangers, or across status lines, is guiltless -- except when a slave kills a free person in self-defense, which is treated as parricide. Forgiveness by the dying victim reduces any homicide to purification and one year of exile. This concludes violent, involuntary, and passionate homicide. We now turn to voluntary, premeditated crimes driven by pleasure, desire, and jealousy."
summary: The Stranger establishes self-defense as a complete justification for homicide across most status categories, except for slaves killing free persons. Deathbed forgiveness by the victim reduces any homicide to purification and one year of exile. He then transitions to voluntary, premeditated crimes.
distilled: Self-defense justifies killing in most cases except slaves killing free persons; deathbed forgiveness reduces any homicide to purification plus one year of exile; the discussion now shifts to premeditated voluntary crime.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The self-defense provisions are notably egalitarian for their time -- citizens, strangers, and even slaves may kill in self-defense without guilt, with the significant exception that a slave may never justifiably kill a free person. This exception reflects the deep status inequality of the slave system: the slave's life is valued less than the free person's under any circumstances. The parallel between a slave killing a free person and parricide suggests that the slave-master relationship is conceived as analogous to the parent-child relationship -- a hierarchical bond so fundamental that it cannot be overridden even by self-preservation. The transition to voluntary, premeditated crime marks a major structural pivot in Book 9: from homicides that are involuntary or committed in passion (which the harm-injustice distinction and rehabilitation can address) to homicides driven by the soul's deliberate submission to pleasure, desire, and jealousy -- the domain of genuine, if still in some sense involuntary, injustice.

narrative:
"Let us first speak, as far as we are able, of their various kinds. The greatest cause of them is lust, which gets the mastery of the soul maddened by desire; and this is most commonly found to exist where the passion reigns which is strongest and most prevalent among the mass of mankind: I mean where the power of wealth breeds endless desires of never-to-be-satisfied acquisition, originating in natural disposition, and a miserable want of education.

"Of this want of education, the false praise of wealth which is bruited about both among Hellenes and barbarians is the cause; they deem that to be the first of goods which in reality is only the third. And in this way they wrong both posterity and themselves, for nothing can be nobler and better than that the truth about wealth should be spoken in all states -- namely, that riches are for the sake of the body, as the body is for the sake of the soul. They are good, and wealth is intended by nature to be for the sake of them, and is therefore inferior to them both, and third in order of excellence.

"This argument teaches us that he who would be happy ought not to seek to be rich, or rather he should seek to be rich justly and temperately, and then there would be no murders in states requiring to be purged away by other murders."
refashioned:
"Let us try to identify the different types of voluntary crime. The greatest cause is greed -- the lust for acquisition that takes command of a soul maddened by desire. This is most prevalent where the strongest and most widespread human passion holds sway: the power of wealth, which breeds insatiable desires for endless accumulation. These desires originate partly from natural temperament but mainly from a deplorable lack of education.

"The root of this educational failure is the false glorification of wealth that prevails among Greeks and non-Greeks alike. People regard wealth as the highest good when in reality it is only the third. This error harms both the present generation and their descendants. The truth that every state should teach is this: wealth exists for the sake of the body, and the body exists for the sake of the soul. Both body and soul are genuine goods, and since wealth exists to serve them, it is naturally inferior to both and ranks third in the order of human goods.

"The lesson is clear: the person who seeks happiness should not pursue wealth as his primary goal. Rather, he should pursue wealth only through just and temperate means. If this principle prevailed, states would not suffer murders that must be purged by yet more murders."
elegant: "The chief cause of voluntary crime is greed -- insatiable desire for wealth, born from poor education and the universal false belief that wealth is the highest good. In truth, wealth ranks third: the soul is first, the body second, and wealth serves both. If people pursued wealth only justly and temperately, states would not require murders to purge earlier murders."
summary: The Stranger identifies greed as the primary cause of voluntary crime. The root problem is educational: both Greeks and non-Greeks falsely rank wealth as the highest good when it is actually third, behind the soul and the body. If people pursued wealth justly and temperately, violent crime would largely cease.
distilled: Greed is the chief cause of voluntary crime, rooted in the universal educational failure of treating wealth as the highest good rather than third after soul and body.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The hierarchy of goods -- soul first, body second, wealth third -- is a foundational Platonic doctrine, stated explicitly in Laws 1 (631b-d) and implicit throughout the Republic (especially 580d-583a, where the three classes of pleasure correspond to soul, honor, and money). The identification of poor education (apaideusia) as the root cause of crime connects directly to the Republic's insistence that justice begins with education (376e-412b). The phrase 'bruited about both among Hellenes and barbarians' universalizes the critique -- this is not a Greek cultural failing but a human one. The closing observation about murders 'requiring to be purged away by other murders' is a powerful image of violence as self-perpetuating cycle, recalling the curse on the house of Atreus and anticipating modern observations about cycles of violence. Compare Gorgias 466c-468e, where Socrates argues that tyrants and the wealthy have the least genuine power because they do what seems best rather than what truly is best.

narrative:
"But now, as I said at first, avarice is the chiefest cause and source of the worst trials for voluntary homicide. A second cause is ambition: this creates jealousies, which are troublesome companions, above all to the jealous man himself, and in a less degree to the chiefs of the state. And a third cause is cowardly and unjust fear, which has been the occasion of many murders. When a man is doing or has done something which he desires that no one should know him to be doing or to have done, he will take the life of those who are likely to inform of such things, if he have no other means of getting rid of them.

"Let this be said as a prelude concerning crimes of violence in general; and I must not omit to mention a tradition which is firmly believed by many, and has been received by them from those who are learned in the mysteries: they say that such deeds will be punished in the world below, and also that when the perpetrators return to this world they will pay the natural penalty which is due to the sufferer, and end their lives in like manner by the hand of another.

"If he who is about to commit murder believes this, and is made by the mere prelude to dread such a penalty, there is no need to proceed with the proclamation of the law. But if he will not listen, let the following law be declared and registered against him:"
refashioned:
"As I said initially, avarice is the primary cause and origin of the most terrible voluntary homicide cases. The second cause is political ambition, which breeds jealousies -- painful primarily to the jealous person himself, but also damaging to the state's leaders. The third cause is cowardly and unjust fear: when someone is engaged in wrongdoing or has already committed it and wants no one to find out, he will kill potential witnesses if he sees no other way to silence them.

"Let this serve as the prelude to our laws about crimes of violence in general. I should also mention a tradition widely and firmly believed, received from those learned in the religious mysteries: that murderers will be punished in the afterlife, and that when they are reborn into this world, they will suffer the same fate they inflicted -- dying by another's hand in the same manner they killed.

"If the would-be murderer believes this tradition and is deterred by the prelude alone, there is no need to proceed to the formal statement of the law. But if he will not listen, let the following law be proclaimed and inscribed against him:"
elegant: "The three causes of voluntary homicide: first, avarice; second, political ambition breeding destructive jealousy; third, cowardly fear that drives people to kill witnesses. As a prelude, the tradition from the mysteries should also be invoked: murderers are punished in the afterlife and, upon rebirth, die by the same means they used to kill. If this deters, the law need not speak. If not, the formal statute follows."
summary: The Stranger identifies three causes of voluntary homicide: avarice, political ambition breeding jealousy, and the cowardly fear that leads to killing witnesses. He invokes the mystery tradition of afterlife punishment and karmic retribution as a prelude. If this religious deterrent fails, the formal law must be declared.
