distilled: Three causes drive voluntary homicide -- avarice, ambitious jealousy, and cowardly fear of exposure -- and the prelude invokes afterlife retribution before stating the law itself.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The three causes of voluntary homicide -- greed, ambition, and fear -- form a concise taxonomy of criminal motivation that remains influential. The third cause (killing to conceal other crimes) is particularly acute and anticipates what modern criminology calls 'instrumental violence.' The appeal to mystery traditions about afterlife punishment and reincarnation reflects the Laws' consistent strategy of incorporating religious belief into legal persuasion. The specific doctrine mentioned -- that murderers are reborn to die in the same manner -- appears to draw on Orphic and Pythagorean teachings about metempsychosis (transmigration of souls), which also influenced the afterlife myths in the Gorgias (523a-527a), Phaedo (113d-114c), and Republic (614b-621d). The pragmatic structure -- prelude first, law only if needed -- enacts the Laws' central principle that persuasion should precede compulsion. The Stranger is not merely asserting the truth of these traditions but using them instrumentally as part of the legislative prelude's persuasive apparatus.

narrative:
"Whoever shall wrongfully and of design slay with his own hand any of his kinsmen, shall in the first place be deprived of legal privileges; and he shall not pollute the temples, or the agora, or the harbours, or any other place of meeting, whether he is forbidden of men or not; for the law, which represents the whole state, forbids him, and always is and will be in the attitude of forbidding him.

"And if a cousin or nearer relative of the deceased, whether on the male or female side, does not prosecute the homicide when he ought, and have him proclaimed an outlaw, he shall in the first place be involved in the pollution, and incur the hatred of the Gods, even as the curse of the law stirs up the voices of men against him; and in the second place he shall be liable to be prosecuted by any one who is willing to inflict retribution on behalf of the dead.

"And he who would avenge a murder shall observe all the precautionary ceremonies of lavation, and any others which the God commands in cases of this kind. Let him have proclamation made, and then go forth and compel the perpetrator to suffer the execution of justice according to the law.

"Now the legislator may easily show that these things must be accomplished by prayers and sacrifices to certain Gods, who are concerned with the prevention of murders in states."
refashioned:
"Whoever deliberately and wrongfully kills a kinsman with his own hand shall first be stripped of all legal rights. He shall not pollute the temples, the marketplace, the harbors, or any other public gathering place -- regardless of whether any person specifically forbids him entry. The law itself, representing the entire state, forbids him and will always forbid him.

"If a cousin or closer relative of the victim, on either the mother's or father's side, fails to prosecute the killer and have him declared an outlaw, that relative shall first incur the pollution himself and attract the hatred of the gods -- the law's curse will turn public opinion against him. Second, he will become liable to prosecution by any citizen willing to seek justice on behalf of the dead.

"Anyone who undertakes to avenge a murder must observe all required purification rituals, including ceremonial washing and whatever else the god prescribes for such cases. He shall have the formal proclamation made, then proceed to compel the murderer to face the law's execution of justice.

"The legislator can readily demonstrate that these proceedings must be accompanied by prayers and sacrifices to those specific gods who are concerned with preventing murder in the state."
elegant: "A deliberate kinsman-killer loses all legal rights and is permanently barred from public spaces by the law itself. Relatives who fail to prosecute absorb the pollution and face prosecution by any citizen. Those who avenge the murder must observe purification rites and formal proclamation before compelling the killer to face justice. The entire process requires prayers and sacrifices to the gods who prevent murder."
summary: The Stranger enacts the law for deliberate kinsman-murder: the killer loses all rights and is barred from public spaces. Relatives who fail to prosecute absorb the pollution. Avengers must observe purification rites before pursuing justice. The proceedings are framed within a religious context of prayer and sacrifice.
distilled: Deliberate kinsman-killers are stripped of legal rights and banned from all public spaces; negligent relatives absorb the pollution; and prosecution must follow prescribed religious purification ceremonies.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The statement that the law 'always is and will be in the attitude of forbidding him' -- even without specific human enforcement -- personifies the law as a moral force independent of any particular enforcer. This reflects the Laws' broader commitment to the rule of law as superior to the rule of individual persons (compare 9.49 on the superiority of law to personal rule). The pollution mechanism that transfers guilt to negligent relatives creates a powerful incentive for prosecution, leveraging family honor and religious fear to ensure enforcement. The requirement for purification before prosecution protects the avenger from contamination by the violence he is about to oversee -- a recognition that even justified involvement with murder carries spiritual risk. The gods 'concerned with the prevention of murders' likely include Apollo (the god of purification, whose Delphic oracle prescribes purification rites) and Zeus Meilichios (Zeus of propitiation, worshipped in connection with blood guilt). These provisions closely parallel historical Athenian homicide law, which required formal proclamation (prorrhesis) by the victim's family.

narrative:
"But who these Gods are, and what should be the true manner of instituting such trials with due regard to religion, the guardians of the law, aided by the interpreters, and the prophets, and the God, shall determine, and when they have determined let them carry on the prosecution at law.

"The cause shall have the same judges who are appointed to decide in the case of those who plunder temples. Let him who is convicted be punished with death, and let him not be buried in the country of the murdered man, for this would be shameless as well as impious.

"But if he fly and will not stand his trial, let him fly for ever; or, if he set foot anywhere on any part of the murdered man's country, let any relation of the deceased, or any other citizen who may first happen to meet with him, kill him with impunity, or bind and deliver him to those among the judges of the case who are magistrates, that they may put him to death.

"And let the prosecutor demand surety of him whom he prosecutes; three sureties sufficient in the opinion of the magistrates who try the cause shall be provided by him, and they shall undertake to produce him at the trial. But if he be unwilling or unable to provide sureties, then the magistrates shall take him and keep him in bonds, and produce him at the day of trial."
refashioned:
"Which gods are relevant and how such trials should be properly conducted with due religious observance shall be determined by the guardians of the law, working together with the religious interpreters, the prophets, and the god himself. Once these procedures are established, prosecution shall proceed accordingly.

"These cases shall be tried by the same judges who handle temple robbery. Anyone convicted shall be executed, and his body shall not be buried anywhere in the murdered person's country -- that would be both shameless and impious.

"If the accused flees and refuses to stand trial, he shall remain a fugitive forever. Should he ever set foot on any part of the victim's country, any relative of the deceased -- or any citizen who happens to encounter him first -- may kill him without penalty, or apprehend him and deliver him to the judicial magistrates for execution.

"The prosecutor shall require the accused to provide three sureties, deemed sufficient by the presiding magistrates, who will guarantee his appearance at trial. If the accused is unwilling or unable to provide sureties, the magistrates shall take him into custody and hold him in bonds until the day of trial."
elegant: "Religious authorities and the guardians of the law shall determine proper trial procedures. The same judges who try temple robbers shall preside. The convicted killer dies and is denied burial in the victim's country. Fugitives may be killed or captured by any citizen. The accused must provide three sureties for trial appearance or be held in custody."
summary: The Stranger specifies trial procedures for premeditated kinsman-murder: religious authorities establish proper procedures, temple-robbery judges preside, conviction means death and denial of local burial, fugitives may be killed by anyone, and the accused must provide sureties or be detained.
distilled: Premeditated kinsman-killers face trial by temple-robbery judges, death and denial of local burial if convicted, and any citizen may kill a fugitive who returns to the victim's country.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The provision allowing any citizen to kill a returned fugitive on sight reflects the ancient Greek institution of outlawry (atimia in its extreme form), where the community collectively withdrew legal protection from the offender. This is not vigilante justice but formal legal sanction: the outlaw has been placed outside the law's protection by the law itself. The surety system (bail) described here closely parallels Athenian legal practice, where sureties (eggutai) guaranteed the defendant's appearance. The denial of burial in the victim's country serves both practical and symbolic purposes: it prevents the murdered person's spirit from being further disturbed by the murderer's proximity (connecting to the ghost beliefs described in 9.30) and symbolizes the murderer's total exclusion from the community he violated. The collaborative determination of religious procedure by guardians of the law, interpreters, prophets, and the god reflects the Laws' consistent integration of human reason and divine guidance in institutional design.

narrative:
"If a man do not commit a murder with his own hand, but contrives the death of another, and is the author of the deed in intention and design, and he continues to dwell in the city, having his soul not pure of the guilt of murder, let him be tried in the same way, except in what relates to the sureties; and also, if he be found guilty, his body after execution may have burial in his native land, but in all other respects his case shall be as the former.

"And whether a stranger shall kill a citizen, or a citizen a stranger, or a slave a slave, there shall be no difference as touching murder by one's own hand or by contrivance, except in the matter of sureties; and these, as has been said, shall be required of the actual murderer only, and he who brings the accusation shall bind them over at the time.

"If a slave be convicted of slaying a freeman voluntarily, either by his own hand or by contrivance, let the public executioner take him in the direction of the sepulchre, to a place whence he can see the tomb of the dead man, and inflict upon him as many stripes as the person who caught him orders, and if he survive, let him put him to death."
refashioned:
"If someone does not commit murder with his own hands but orchestrates another person's death -- being the author of the killing in intention and design -- and continues living in the city with a soul polluted by the guilt of murder, he shall be tried under the same procedures, with one exception regarding sureties. Additionally, if convicted, his body may be buried in his native land after execution. In all other respects, his case is treated identically to murder by one's own hand.

"Whether a stranger kills a citizen, a citizen kills a stranger, or a slave kills a slave, there shall be no distinction between murder committed personally and murder committed by contrivance, except regarding sureties. As stated, sureties are required only of the person who physically committed the killing; the prosecutor shall secure them at the time charges are brought.

"If a slave is convicted of voluntarily killing a free person, whether by his own hand or through contrivance, the public executioner shall take him near the victim's tomb -- to a place where he can see the burial site -- and shall flog him with as many stripes as the person who captured him orders. If the slave survives the flogging, the executioner shall put him to death."
elegant: "Conspiracy to murder is tried the same as murder by one's own hand, except the conspirator may be buried in his native land if convicted. Sureties are required only of the physical killer. These rules apply regardless of status -- citizen, stranger, or slave. A slave convicted of voluntarily killing a free person is flogged within sight of the victim's tomb and then executed."
summary: The Stranger extends murder law to cover conspiracy and contrivance, treating it nearly identically to direct killing. Rules apply across all status categories. Sureties are required only of the physical killer. Slaves convicted of voluntarily killing free persons are flogged in sight of the victim's tomb and then executed.
distilled: Murder by contrivance is treated the same as murder by one's own hand regardless of the parties' status, with sureties required only of the physical killer.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The extension of murder liability to those who 'contrive' (bouleusas) a death -- the masterminds of murder -- reflects a principle also found in Athenian law, which tried conspirators and those who planned murders alongside the actual killers. The Athenian court at the Palladion handled cases of bouleusis (planning or instigation of murder). The slight distinction in punishment -- the conspirator may be buried in his native land while the direct killer may not -- perhaps reflects a judgment that while the moral guilt is equivalent, the physical act of killing creates a different kind of pollution. The brutal punishment prescribed for slaves who kill free persons -- flogging within sight of the victim's tomb followed by execution -- combines retributive elements (the slave sees the consequence of his act) with the status-based severity that characterizes the Laws' treatment of slave criminality throughout Book 9.

narrative:
"And if any one kills a slave who has done no wrong, because he is afraid that he may inform of some base and evil deeds of his own, or for any similar reason, in such a case let him pay the penalty of murder, as he would have done if he had slain a citizen.

"There are things about which it is terrible and unpleasant to legislate, but impossible not to legislate. If, for example, there should be murders of kinsmen, either perpetrated by the hands of kinsmen, or by their contrivance, voluntary and purely malicious, which most often happen in ill-regulated and ill-educated states, and may perhaps occur even in a country where a man would not expect to find them, we must repeat once more the tale which we narrated a little while ago, in the hope that he who hears us will be the more disposed to abstain voluntarily on these grounds from murders which are utterly abominable.

"For the myth, or saying, or whatever we ought to call it, has been plainly set forth by priests of old; they have pronounced that the justice which guards and avenges the blood of kindred follows the law of retaliation, and ordains that he who has done any murderous act should of necessity suffer that which he has done. He who has slain a father shall himself be slain at some time or other by his children -- if a mother, he shall of necessity take a woman's nature, and lose his life at the hands of his offspring in after ages; for where the blood of a family has been polluted there is no other purification, nor can the pollution be washed out until the homicidal soul which did the deed has given life for life, and has propitiated and laid to sleep the wrath of the whole family. These are the retributions of Heaven, and by such punishments men should be deterred."
refashioned:
"If someone kills an innocent slave out of fear that the slave might expose his wrongdoing, or for any similar reason, he shall pay the full penalty for murder, exactly as if he had killed a citizen.

"Some crimes are terrible and unpleasant to legislate about, yet legislation is unavoidable. For example, deliberate and malicious murders among family members -- committed by kinsmen's own hands or through their scheming -- occur most often in poorly governed and poorly educated states, but may arise even in places where one would never expect them. We must therefore repeat the ancient tradition mentioned earlier, hoping that those who hear it will be more inclined to abstain voluntarily from these utterly abominable acts.

"The myth -- or teaching, or whatever we should call it -- was clearly stated by priests long ago: the divine justice that guards and avenges the blood of kinsmen operates by the law of retaliation. Whoever commits murder must inevitably suffer the same fate. He who kills his father will someday be killed by his own children. He who kills his mother must in a future life take on a woman's nature and die at the hands of his offspring in a later age. Once a family's blood has been polluted, there is no purification except life given for life -- the homicidal soul must repay in kind until the wrath of the entire family line is appeased and laid to rest. These are Heaven's retributions, and they should deter anyone considering such acts."
elegant: "Killing an innocent slave to silence him carries the same penalty as killing a citizen. As for deliberate family murders -- terrible to legislate but impossible to ignore -- the ancient priestly tradition teaches that divine retribution follows the law of retaliation: a father-killer will be killed by his children, a mother-killer will be reborn as a woman and killed by offspring in a future life. Family blood pollution can only be purified by life given for life."
summary: The Stranger rules that killing an innocent slave to prevent exposure carries the full murder penalty. He then addresses premeditated family murder, invoking the ancient tradition of divine retribution: killers of parents will be killed by their own children, either in this life or through reincarnation, until the blood pollution is purged life for life.
distilled: Killing a slave to silence him carries the full murder penalty; premeditated family murder is deterred by the priestly teaching that divine justice demands life for life across generations and reincarnations.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The provision treating the murder of an innocent slave as equivalent to the murder of a citizen is remarkably progressive for ancient legislation and directly applies the Stranger's earlier principle (9.22) that murder for fear of exposure is one of the three causes of voluntary homicide. The retaliatory theology draws heavily on Orphic and Pythagorean doctrines of metempsychosis. The specific detail that a mother-killer must 'take a woman's nature' in a future life reflects the belief that karmic justice operates with exact symmetry -- the punishment mirrors the crime. The Stranger's hedging about what to call this teaching -- 'myth, or saying, or whatever we ought to call it' -- is characteristic of Plato's careful handling of religious traditions that he finds useful for moral persuasion without necessarily affirming their literal truth. Compare the Phaedo's treatment of afterlife myths (114d): 'No sensible man would insist that these things are exactly as I have described... but that either this or something like it is true about our souls and their dwelling places... this seems to me fitting and worth risking.'

narrative:
"But if they are not deterred, and any one should be incited by some fatality to deprive his father or mother, or brethren, or children, of life voluntarily and of purpose, for him the earthly lawgiver legislates as follows:

"There shall be the same proclamations about outlawry, and there shall be the same sureties which have been enacted in the former cases. But in his case, if he be convicted, the servants of the judges and the magistrates shall slay him at an appointed place without the city where three ways meet, and there expose his body naked, and each of the magistrates on behalf of the whole city shall take a stone and cast it upon the head of the dead man, and so deliver the city from pollution; after that, they shall bear him to the borders of the land, and cast him forth unburied, according to law.

"And what shall he suffer who slays him who of all men, as they say, is his own best friend? I mean the suicide, who deprives himself by violence of his appointed share of life, not because the law of the state requires him, nor yet under the compulsion of some painful and inevitable misfortune which has come upon him, nor because he has had to suffer from irremediable and intolerable shame, but who from sloth or want of manliness imposes upon himself an unjust penalty."
refashioned:
"But if the religious prelude fails to deter, and someone is driven by fate to deliberately and purposefully kill his father, mother, siblings, or children, the earthly legislator prescribes as follows:

"The same proclamations of outlawry and the same surety requirements from previous cases shall apply. If convicted, the servants of the judges and magistrates shall execute him at a designated place outside the city where three roads meet. There they shall expose his body naked, and each magistrate, acting on behalf of the entire city, shall cast a stone upon the dead man's head, thereby delivering the city from pollution. Afterward, they shall carry his body to the borders and cast it forth unburied, as the law requires.

"And what of the person who kills his own greatest friend -- himself? I mean the suicide, who violently deprives himself of his allotted portion of life. I am not speaking of one compelled by state law, or driven by some painful and unavoidable catastrophe, or forced by irremediable and intolerable shame. I mean the person who, out of mere laziness or lack of courage, imposes upon himself an unjust penalty."
elegant: "If religious deterrence fails: the convicted family-killer is executed outside the city at a crossroads, his body exposed naked, and each magistrate casts a stone on the corpse to purge the city's pollution. The body is then carried to the borders and cast out unburied. Next, the question of suicide: one who kills himself not from necessity, catastrophe, or unbearable shame, but from laziness or cowardice, imposes an unjust penalty on himself."
summary: For convicted family murderers, the law prescribes execution at a crossroads, public stoning of the corpse by magistrates to purge pollution, and denial of burial. The Stranger then raises suicide, distinguishing justified cases (state compulsion, unavoidable suffering, irremediable shame) from unjustified ones (laziness and cowardice).
distilled: Convicted family murderers are executed at a crossroads and their bodies are stoned by magistrates and cast out unburied; suicide from laziness or cowardice -- but not from necessity or unbearable suffering -- is an unjust self-penalty.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The execution at the crossroads (triodos) where three roads meet is rich with religious significance. Crossroads were associated with Hecate and with the disposal of pollution in Greek religion. The ritual stoning of the corpse by magistrates is not punishment of the dead but a communal purification -- each official, acting for the whole city, symbolically expels the pollution. This recalls the scapegoat (pharmakos) rituals in which Greek cities ceremonially expelled a polluted figure. The transition to suicide is philosophically significant. Plato's treatment of suicide here is more nuanced than is often noted: he distinguishes three legitimate grounds (state requirement, unavoidable misfortune, intolerable shame) from the illegitimate ground of 'sloth or want of manliness.' This connects to the Phaedo (61c-62c), where Socrates argues that we should not kill ourselves because we are the gods' possessions and must await their summons -- yet Socrates himself faces death willingly when the state requires it. The phrase 'his own best friend' applied to the self echoes the Aristotelian dictum (Nicomachean Ethics 9.4) that a person's relationship to himself is the model for friendship.

narrative:
"For him, what ceremonies there are to be of purification and burial God knows, and about these the next of kin should enquire of the interpreters and of the laws thereto relating, and do according to their injunctions.

"They who meet their death in this way shall be buried alone, and none shall be laid by their side; they shall be buried ingloriously in the borders of the twelve portions of the land, in such places as are uncultivated and nameless, and no column or inscription shall mark the place of their interment.

"And if a beast of burden or other animal cause the death of any one, except in the case of anything of that kind happening to a competitor in the public contests, the kinsmen of the deceased shall prosecute the slayer for murder, and the wardens of the country, such and so many as the kinsmen appoint, shall try the cause, and let the beast when condemned be slain by them, and let them cast it beyond the borders.

"And if any lifeless thing deprive a man of life, except in the case of a thunderbolt or other fatal dart sent from the Gods -- whether a man is killed by lifeless objects falling upon him, or by his falling upon them -- the nearest of kin shall appoint the nearest neighbour to be a judge, and thereby acquit himself and the whole family of guilt. And he shall cast forth the guilty thing beyond the border, as has been said about the animals."
refashioned:
"As for the suicide's purification and burial ceremonies -- that is a matter for God to determine. The next of kin should consult the religious interpreters and the relevant laws and follow their instructions.

"Those who die by their own hand shall be buried alone with no one beside them. They shall be interred ingloriously on the boundaries of the twelve land divisions, in uncultivated and unnamed places. No column or inscription shall mark their burial site.

"If a beast of burden or other animal causes someone's death -- except during public competitions -- the victim's kinsmen shall prosecute the animal for murder. The countryside wardens, as many as the kinsmen designate, shall try the case. If the animal is found guilty, they shall kill it and cast it beyond the borders.

"If a lifeless object causes death -- except for a thunderbolt or other divine missile -- whether by falling on the person or by the person falling onto it, the nearest kin shall appoint the nearest neighbor as judge. This procedure acquits the kin and the entire family of guilt. The 'guilty' object shall then be cast beyond the borders, just as with animals."
elegant: "Suicides are buried alone, without markers, in nameless uncultivated land on the borders. Animals that cause death are tried for murder and, if convicted, killed and expelled beyond the borders. Lifeless objects that cause death -- except divine instruments like thunderbolts -- are ceremonially expelled after a neighbor serves as judge, acquitting the family of guilt."
summary: The Stranger prescribes inglorious, unmarked burial for suicides. Animals that cause death are formally tried and expelled. Lifeless objects that cause death (except divine instruments) undergo a similar ritual expulsion, with a neighbor serving as judge to acquit the victim's family of pollution.
distilled: Suicides receive isolated, unmarked burial; death-causing animals are tried and expelled; and death-causing objects are ceremonially cast out to acquit the family of pollution.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The treatment of suicides -- solitary burial, no markers, unnamed locations -- reflects a combination of moral disapproval and practical pollution management. The suicide is neither honored nor publicly shamed; he is simply erased from communal memory. This stands between the honor burial of the good citizen and the unburied exposure of the criminal. The trial and execution of animals and objects that cause death may seem bizarre to modern readers but was genuine Greek legal practice. Athenian law, administered through the court at the Prytaneion, tried animals and inanimate objects for causing death. The procedure served an important function: by formally identifying and expelling the 'guilty' agent, the community purified itself of the pollution associated with violent death. The exception for thunderbolts and other 'darts sent from the Gods' acknowledges divine agency as exempt from human jurisdiction -- an important theological boundary. Compare Aristotle's discussion of the Prytaneion court (Constitution of Athens 57.4) and Aeschines' reference to the trial of inanimate objects (Against Ctesiphon 244).

narrative:
"If a man is found dead, and his murderer be unknown, and after a diligent search cannot be detected, there shall be the same proclamation as in the previous cases, and the same interdict on the murderer; and having proceeded against him, they shall proclaim in the agora by a herald, that he who has slain such and such a person, and has been convicted of murder, shall not set his foot in the temples, nor at all in the country of the murdered man, and if he appears and is discovered, he shall die, and be cast forth unburied beyond the border.

"Let this one law then be laid down by us about murder; and let cases of this sort be so regarded."
refashioned:
"If a person is found dead and the killer remains unknown despite a thorough investigation, the same proclamation and interdiction shall apply as in previous cases. Having formally proceeded against the unknown killer, the authorities shall announce by herald in the marketplace: 'Whoever killed this person, having been convicted of murder in absentia, shall not set foot in any temple or anywhere in the murdered person's country. If he appears and is identified, he shall be put to death and his body cast out unburied beyond the borders.'

"Let this be our final word on murder law, and let all such cases be handled accordingly."
elegant: "When a killer remains unknown despite investigation, the same proclamation and banishment apply. A herald announces in the marketplace that the unidentified killer is banned from temples and the victim's country; if discovered, he faces death and denial of burial. This concludes the law of murder."
summary: The Stranger addresses unsolved murders: the standard proclamation and banishment apply to the unknown killer, announced publicly by herald. Discovery means death and denial of burial. This passage closes the homicide section of Book 9.
distilled: For unsolved murders, the unknown killer is formally proclaimed an outlaw by herald, banned from temples and the victim's country, and subject to death if later discovered.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This provision for unsolved murders reflects the Greek conviction that blood pollution must be addressed even when the killer is unknown. Athenian law similarly provided for proclamations against unknown murderers, issued at the Prytaneion. The formal proclamation by herald (kerux) in the agora -- the public marketplace and civic center -- ensures maximum publicity. The legal fiction of proceeding against an unknown killer served the important psychological and religious function of demonstrating that the community had not acquiesced in the murder. By formally declaring the unknown killer an outlaw, the city affirms its commitment to justice even when practical enforcement is impossible. This passage also provides a clear structural marker: 'Let this one law then be laid down by us about murder' signals the transition from homicide to the next category of violent crime (wounding and assault).

narrative:
"And now let us say in what cases and under what circumstances the murderer is rightly free from guilt: If a man catch a thief coming into his house by night to steal, and he take and kill him, or if he slay a footpad in self-defence, he shall be guiltless.

"And any one who does violence to a free woman or a youth shall be slain with impunity by the injured person, or by his or her father or brothers or sons. If a man find his wife suffering violence, he may kill the violator, and be guiltless in the eye of the law; or if a person kill another in warding off death from his father or mother or children or brethren or wife who are doing no wrong, he shall assuredly be guiltless."
refashioned:
"Now let us specify the cases where killing is justifiable and the killer is rightly free from guilt.

"If someone catches a thief breaking into his house at night and kills him, or if he kills a highway robber in self-defense, he shall be guiltless.

"Anyone who sexually assaults a free woman or a young person may be killed with impunity by the victim, or by the victim's father, brothers, or sons. If a man discovers someone assaulting his wife, he may kill the attacker and stand guiltless before the law. And if anyone kills another person while defending the life of a parent, child, sibling, or spouse who is innocent of wrongdoing, that person shall be entirely guiltless."
elegant: "Justifiable homicide: killing a nighttime burglar or a highway robber in self-defense. Killing a rapist -- by the victim or the victim's father, brothers, or sons. Killing someone assaulting one's wife. Killing in defense of the life of an innocent parent, child, sibling, or spouse. All guiltless."
summary: The Stranger establishes the categories of justifiable homicide: killing burglars and highway robbers in self-defense, killing sexual assailants, and killing to defend innocent family members from lethal threat.
distilled: Killing in defense of home, person, or innocent family members -- including killing burglars, robbers, rapists, and those threatening the lives of kin -- is entirely guiltless.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. These justifiable homicide provisions closely parallel Athenian law. Draco's homicide law (as preserved in the re-inscription of 409/8 BCE) included provisions for killing in self-defense, and Athenian law specifically permitted killing a man caught in the act of sexual violence against one's wife, mother, sister, daughter, or concubine (as described in Lysias' speech On the Murder of Eratosthenes). The right to kill in defense of family members extends the self-defense principle beyond the individual to the household (oikos), which was the fundamental social unit of Greek life. The specification that the protected family members must be 'doing no wrong' prevents the justification from being used in cases where the 'victim' was actually an aggressor. These provisions represent the law's recognition that some situations require immediate violent action and that the legal system cannot always be invoked in time to protect life and honor.

narrative:
"Thus much as to the nurture and education of the living soul of man, having which, he can, and without which, if he unfortunately be without them, he cannot live; and also concerning the punishments which are to be inflicted for violent deaths, let thus much be enacted.

"Of the nurture and education of the body we have spoken before, and next in order we have to speak of deeds of violence, voluntary and involuntary, which men do to one another; these we will now distinguish, as far as we are able, according to their nature and number, and determine what will be the suitable penalties of each, and so assign to them their proper place in the series of our enactments.

"The poorest legislator will have no difficulty in determining that wounds and mutilations arising out of wounds should follow next in order after deaths. Let wounds be divided as homicides were divided -- into those which are involuntary, and which are given in passion or from fear, and those inflicted voluntarily and with premeditation.

"Concerning all this, we must make some such proclamation as the following: Mankind must have laws, and conform to them, or their life would be as bad as that of the most savage beast. And the reason of this is that no man's nature is able to know what is best for human society; or knowing, always able and willing to do what is best."
refashioned:
"We have now covered the nurture and education of the human soul -- without which a person cannot truly live -- as well as the penalties for violent death. Let this much be enacted.

"We have previously discussed the care of the body, and the next topic in order is violent acts that people commit against one another, both voluntary and involuntary. We will classify these as precisely as possible according to their nature and variety, determine appropriate penalties for each, and assign them their proper place in our legal code.

"Even the most basic legislator can see that wounds and mutilations should naturally follow deaths in our sequence. Wounds shall be categorized the same way as homicides: involuntary, passionate or fear-driven, and voluntary with premeditation.

"Before proceeding, a fundamental proclamation is necessary: Human beings must have laws and obey them, or their lives would be no better than the most savage beasts. The reason is that no person's natural capacity is sufficient to know what is truly best for human society -- or, even if he knows, to remain always able and willing to do what is best."
elegant: "Having covered homicide law, we turn to wounding and mutilation, classified like homicide into involuntary, passionate, and premeditated categories. A foundational proclamation: humans must have laws or become beasts. No one naturally knows what is best for society, and even those who know cannot always will themselves to act on that knowledge."
summary: The Stranger transitions from homicide to wounding and assault, using the same classification system. He prefaces this section with a fundamental claim: humans need laws because no one's natural capacity is sufficient to consistently know and do what is best for society.
distilled: Wounding and mutilation are classified like homicide; the foundational principle is that humans need laws because no one can naturally and consistently know and do what is best for society.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The proclamation that 'mankind must have laws, or their life would be as bad as that of the most savage beast' is one of the Laws' most quoted passages and represents a fundamental departure from the Republic's aspiration to governance by perfect knowledge rather than law. The two-part argument -- that humans cannot reliably know the good, and even knowing it, cannot reliably act on it -- combines the epistemological problem (ignorance) with the motivational problem (weakness of will, or akrasia). The Republic famously denies the possibility of akrasia (following Protagoras 352b-358d), but the Laws here implicitly acknowledges it as a practical reality. This admission is central to understanding why the Laws turns from philosopher-kings to law: the 'second-best' regime relies on fixed rules precisely because no human can be trusted to exercise perfect judgment consistently. Compare Aristotle's response in Politics 3.15-16 on whether it is better to be ruled by the best laws or the best man.

narrative:
"In the first place, there is a difficulty in apprehending that the true art of politics is concerned, not with private but with public good -- for public good binds together states, but private only distracts them -- and that both the public and private good as well of individuals as of states is greater when the state and not the individual is first considered.

"In the second place, although a person knows in the abstract that this is true, yet if he be possessed of absolute and irresponsible power, he will never remain firm in his principles or persist in regarding the public good as primary in the state, and the private good as secondary. Human nature will be always drawing him into avarice and selfishness, avoiding pain and pursuing pleasure without any reason, and will bring these to the front, obscuring the juster and better; and so working darkness in his soul will at last fill with evils both him and the whole city.

"For if a man were born so divinely gifted that he could naturally apprehend the truth, he would have no need of laws to rule over him; for there is no law or order which is above knowledge, nor can mind, without impiety, be deemed the subject or slave of any man, but rather the lord of all. I speak of mind, true and free, and in harmony with nature. But then there is no such mind anywhere, or at least not much; and therefore we must choose law and order, which are second best. These look at things as they exist for the most part only, and are unable to survey the whole of them. And therefore I have spoken as I have."
refashioned:
"The first difficulty is recognizing that the true art of politics concerns public good, not private good -- for public good unites states while private interest tears them apart -- and that both public and private welfare are actually greater when the state's interests are placed above the individual's.

"The second difficulty is this: even when someone understands this truth in the abstract, if given absolute and unchecked power, he will never consistently prioritize public good over private. Human nature will constantly pull him toward greed and selfishness, toward irrational avoidance of pain and pursuit of pleasure. These impulses will push their way to the front of his mind, obscuring what is just and good. The resulting darkness in his soul will eventually fill both him and the entire city with evils.

"Now, if anyone were so divinely gifted as to naturally grasp the truth, he would need no laws to govern him. No law or institution is superior to genuine knowledge, and it would be impious to make true, free mind -- mind in harmony with nature -- the subject or slave of anything. Mind should be lord of all. But such a mind does not exist anywhere, or at least is exceedingly rare. Therefore we must settle for law and order, which are second best. Laws can only address how things usually are; they cannot encompass every particular situation. That is why I have argued as I have."
elegant: "True politics serves public good, not private -- yet even someone who knows this will, given absolute power, inevitably be corrupted by human nature's pull toward selfishness and pleasure. A perfectly wise mind would need no laws and should be lord of all. But such minds are essentially nonexistent. So we settle for law -- second best, covering only the general case, unable to survey every particular. This is why we need legislation."
summary: The Stranger argues that true politics serves public over private good, but absolute power corrupts even those who know this. A mind with perfect knowledge would rightfully rule without law, but since such minds are virtually nonexistent, law is the necessary second-best solution -- general rules that cannot cover every particular case.
distilled: Since no human mind can reliably know and pursue the public good when given unchecked power, law -- though merely a second-best approximation that cannot cover every particular -- is humanity's necessary recourse.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage is one of the most philosophically significant in the entire Laws and marks Plato's mature position on the relationship between knowledge and political authority. In the Republic (473c-e), philosopher-kings rule by knowledge, making law almost superfluous. Here, the Stranger explicitly declares law 'second best' to knowledge but necessary because perfect knowledge does not exist in practice. The statement that 'there is no law or order which is above knowledge, nor can mind, without impiety, be deemed the subject or slave of any man' preserves the theoretical superiority of the Republic's ideal while conceding its practical impossibility. The analysis of how absolute power corrupts anticipates Lord Acton by two millennia and echoes the Republic's own analysis of the tyrant's soul (571a-576b). The concept of law as dealing only with 'things as they exist for the most part' connects to Aristotle's later discussion of equity (epieikeia) in Nicomachean Ethics 5.10 -- the recognition that general rules cannot anticipate every particular situation, requiring judicial discretion to fill the gaps. Compare also Statesman 294a-300c on the relationship between true statesmanship and written law.

narrative:
"And now we will determine what penalty he ought to pay or suffer who has hurt or wounded another. Any one may easily imagine the questions which have to be asked in all such cases: What did he wound, or whom, or how, or when? For there are innumerable particulars of this sort which greatly vary from one another. And to allow courts of law to determine all these things, or not to determine any of them, is alike impossible. There is one particular which they must determine in all cases -- the question of fact.

"And then, again, that the legislator should not permit them to determine what punishment is to be inflicted in any of these cases, but should himself decide about all of them, small or great, is next to impossible."

"Then what is to be the inference?"

"The inference is, that some things should be left to courts of law; others the legislator must decide for himself."

"And what ought the legislator to decide, and what ought he to leave to courts of law?"
refashioned:
"Now let us determine the penalties for hurting or wounding another person. The questions to be asked in each case are easy to imagine: What was wounded? Who was the victim? How was the wound inflicted? When did it happen? The particulars are innumerable and vary enormously from case to case. It is equally impossible to have courts determine all of these matters and to have them determine none.

"One thing courts must always determine: the factual question of what actually happened. But for the legislator to personally decide the punishment in every single case, large or small, is nearly impossible."

"What follows from this?"

"That some matters must be left to courts, while others the legislator must decide himself."

"Which matters should the legislator decide, and which should he leave to the courts?"
elegant: "Wounding cases involve countless variables -- what, who, how, when. Courts cannot determine everything, nor can they be denied all judgment. They must always decide the question of fact. But the legislator cannot prescribe the exact penalty for every possible case. The solution: divide the work. Some things the legislator decides; others he leaves to the courts."
summary: The Stranger addresses the practical problem of legislating for wounding cases: the infinite variety of circumstances makes it impossible for either the legislator or the courts to handle everything alone. Courts must determine facts; the allocation of sentencing authority between legislator and courts requires careful division.
distilled: The infinite variety of wounding cases requires dividing authority between legislator and courts: courts determine facts, while sentencing is shared between predetermined rules and judicial discretion.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage addresses a perennial problem in legal philosophy: the relationship between legislative prescription and judicial discretion. The Stranger's solution -- that courts must determine facts while the legislator prescribes some penalties and leaves others to judicial judgment -- anticipates the modern distinction between mandatory and discretionary sentencing. The underlying principle from 9.49 informs this: since law deals only with generalities while cases involve infinite particulars, some flexibility must be built into the system. The Stranger's inclusion of a dialogical question ('What is the inference?') within his own speech creates the impression of thinking through the problem in real time, a technique characteristic of the Socratic method even when the Stranger is speaking at length. Compare Aristotle's discussion of the necessity for equity (epieikeia) as a correction to law's generality (Nicomachean Ethics 5.10, 1137b-1138a).

narrative:
"I may reply, that in a state in which the courts are bad and mute, because the judges conceal their opinions and decide causes clandestinely; or what is worse, when they are disorderly and noisy, as in a theatre, clapping or hooting in turn this or that orator -- I say that then there is a very serious evil, which affects the whole state.

"Unfortunate is the necessity of having to legislate for such courts, but where the necessity exists, the legislator should only allow them to ordain the penalties for the smallest offences; if the state for which he is legislating be of this character, he must take most matters into his own hands and speak distinctly.

"But when a state has good courts, and the judges are well trained and scrupulously tested, the determination of the penalties or punishments which shall be inflicted on the guilty may fairly and with advantage be left to them. And we are not to be blamed for not legislating concerning all that large class of matters which judges far worse educated than ours would be able to determine, assigning to each offence what is due both to the perpetrator and to the sufferer.

"We believe those for whom we are legislating to be best able to judge, and therefore to them the greater part may be left. At the same time, as I have often said, we should exhibit to the judges, as we have done, the outline and form of the punishments to be inflicted, and then they will not transgress the just rule. That was an excellent practice, which we observed before, and which now that we are resuming the work of legislation, may with advantage be repeated by us."
refashioned:
"It depends on the quality of the courts. In a state where judges conceal their opinions and decide cases secretly, or worse, where the court is disorderly and noisy like a theater audience, cheering or jeering at each speaker in turn -- that is a grave evil affecting the entire state. A legislator forced to work with such courts should allow them to set penalties only for the most trivial offenses and should prescribe everything else himself.

"But in a state with good courts, where judges are well trained and rigorously tested, it is both fair and advantageous to let them determine punishments for the guilty. We should not be criticized for leaving to judicial discretion the many cases that judges far less educated than ours could handle competently, matching penalties to what is due both the offender and the victim.

"Since we believe our judges will be the best qualified to assess individual cases, we can entrust them with the greater part of sentencing. But as I have said many times, we should provide them with the general outline and framework of appropriate penalties. With these guidelines in hand, they will not depart from just standards. This is the excellent practice we followed before and should continue as we resume our legislation."
elegant: "Bad courts -- secretive or theatrical -- should have minimal sentencing power; the legislator must prescribe most penalties himself. Good courts with well-trained judges can be trusted with broad sentencing discretion. Since our judges will be excellently qualified, we leave them most determinations while providing the outline and framework of just penalties as a guide."
summary: The Stranger's answer depends on court quality: bad courts should have sentencing authority restricted to trivial cases, while good courts with trained judges can be trusted with broad discretion. For their state, well-qualified judges will handle most sentencing, guided by the legislator's general framework.
distilled: Sentencing discretion should be proportional to court quality: bad courts deserve minimal authority, while well-trained judges deserve broad discretion guided by legislative frameworks.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The comparison of bad courts to a noisy theater audience is a vivid critique of democratic legal practice. Athenian courts (dikasteria) were notorious for audience-like behavior -- large juries of hundreds of citizens would react vocally to speakers, and judicial decisions could be influenced by rhetorical skill more than by the merits of the case. This criticism echoes the Gorgias' attack on rhetoric as flattery rather than genuine art (462b-466a) and the Republic's concern about the influence of popular opinion on judgment (492a-493d). The Stranger's solution -- matching judicial discretion to institutional quality -- is a sophisticated institutional design principle. The concept of providing judges with an 'outline and form' (tupon kai schama) of appropriate punishments while leaving specific application to their judgment anticipates modern sentencing guidelines, which provide frameworks while allowing judicial discretion within established parameters.

narrative:
"Let the enactment about wounding be in the following terms: If anyone has a purpose and intention to slay another who is not his enemy, and whom the law does not permit him to slay, and he wounds him, but is unable to kill him, he who had the intent and has wounded him is not to be pitied -- he deserves no consideration, but should be regarded as a murderer and be tried for murder.

"Still having respect to the fortune which has in a manner favoured him, and to the providence which in pity to him and to the wounded man saved the one from a fatal blow, and the other from an accursed fate and calamity -- as a thank-offering to this deity, and in order not to oppose his will -- in such a case the law will remit the punishment of death, and only compel the offender to emigrate to a neighbouring city for the rest of his life, where he shall remain in the enjoyment of all his possessions.

"But if he have injured the wounded man, he shall make such compensation for the injury as the court deciding the cause shall assess, and the same judges shall decide who would have decided if the man had died of his wounds.

"And if a child intentionally wound his parents, or a servant his master, death shall be the penalty. And if a brother or a sister intentionally wound a brother or a sister, and is found guilty, death shall be the penalty. And if a husband wound a wife, or a wife a husband, with intent to kill, let him or her undergo perpetual exile; if they have sons or daughters who are still young, the guardians shall take care of their property, and have charge of the children as orphans. If their sons are grown up, they shall be under no obligation to support the exiled parent, but they shall possess the property themselves."
refashioned:
"The law on wounding shall be as follows: If someone intends and attempts to kill a person who is not an enemy and whom the law does not authorize him to kill, but succeeds only in wounding rather than killing -- that person deserves no pity. He should be treated as a murderer and tried for murder.

"However, out of gratitude to the good fortune -- or divine providence -- that mercifully spared the attacker from delivering a fatal blow and the victim from a cursed death, the law will not impose the death penalty. Instead, the offender shall be permanently exiled to a neighboring city, retaining full enjoyment of his property.

"He must also compensate the wounded person for injuries, as assessed by the court that would have tried the case had the victim died.

"If a child intentionally wounds a parent, or a servant his master, the penalty is death. If a brother or sister intentionally wounds a sibling and is convicted, the penalty is death. If a husband wounds his wife, or a wife her husband, with intent to kill, the penalty is permanent exile. If they have young children, the guardians shall manage the property and care for the children as orphans. If the children are grown, they owe no support to the exiled parent and shall keep the property themselves."
elegant: "Attempted murder (wounding with intent to kill) is tried as murder but, in gratitude for the fortune that prevented a death, the penalty is permanent exile rather than execution. Compensation for injuries is additionally assessed. Children who wound parents, or servants who wound masters: death. Siblings who wound siblings with intent: death. Spouses who wound spouses with intent to kill: permanent exile, with children treated as orphans."
summary: The Stranger treats attempted murder as morally equivalent to murder but reduces the penalty to permanent exile in deference to the fortune that prevented death. Additional compensation for injuries is required. Special cases: wounding parents or masters carries death; spousal assault with intent to kill means permanent exile with children treated as orphans.
distilled: Attempted murder is tried as murder but punished by permanent exile rather than death in gratitude for the fortune that prevented the killing; wounding parents or masters carries the death penalty.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The treatment of attempted murder as morally equivalent to completed murder is philosophically consistent with the Laws' focus on the soul's condition (intention) rather than external outcomes (9.26). The reduction of penalty from death to exile is justified not by the attacker's lesser guilt but by gratitude to divine providence -- a characteristically religious rationale. This creates an elegant compromise: the law acknowledges equal moral culpability while pragmatically recognizing that the actual harm was less. The special severity for children wounding parents and servants wounding masters reflects the hierarchical values that pervade the Laws' social vision. The spousal wounding provisions are again gender-neutral (husband wounding wife and wife wounding husband are treated identically), and the treatment of children as orphans when a parent is exiled for attempted spousal murder is a practical provision that prioritizes the children's welfare over the criminal parent's rights.

narrative:
"And if he who meets with such a misfortune has no children, the kindred of the exiled man to the degree of sons of cousins, both on the male and female side, shall meet together, and after taking counsel with the guardians of the law and the priests, shall appoint a 5040th citizen to be the heir of the house, considering and reasoning that no house of all the 5040 belongs to the inhabitant or to the whole family, but is the public and private property of the state.

"Now the state should seek to have its houses as holy and happy as possible. And if any one of the houses be unfortunate, and stained with impiety, and the owner leave no posterity, but dies unmarried, or married and childless, having suffered death as the penalty of murder or some other crime committed against the Gods or against his fellow-citizens, of which death is the penalty distinctly laid down in the law; or if any of the citizens be in perpetual exile, and also childless, that house shall first of all be purified and undergo expiation according to law; and then let the kinsmen of the house, as we were just now saying, and the guardians of the law, meet and consider what family there is in the state which is of the highest repute for virtue and also for good fortune, in which there are a number of sons; from that family let them take one and introduce him to the father and forefathers of the dead man as their son, and, for the sake of the omen, let him be called so, that he may be the continuer of their family, the keeper of their hearth, and the minister of their sacred rites with better fortune than his father had; and when they have made this supplication, they shall make him heir according to law, and the offending person they shall leave nameless and childless and portionless when calamities such as these overtake him."
refashioned:
"If the exiled person has no children, his relatives up to the degree of second cousins, on both the maternal and paternal sides, shall assemble. In consultation with the guardians of the law and the priests, they shall appoint someone to become the 5,040th citizen and heir to the vacant house. The fundamental principle is that no household among the 5,040 belongs exclusively to its inhabitant or family; each is simultaneously public and private property of the state.

"The state should strive to make every household as sacred and prosperous as possible. When a household is unfortunate -- stained by impiety, its owner dead or exiled without leaving heirs, whether through execution for murder or other capital crimes against the gods or fellow citizens, or through childless perpetual exile -- the house must first be purified and undergo legal expiation.

"Then the kinsmen and the guardians of the law shall identify the family in the state most distinguished for both virtue and good fortune, one that has several sons. They shall take one son from that family and formally adopt him as the child of the failed household's ancestors. For the sake of the omen, he shall bear the family name and serve as the continuer of their line, keeper of their hearth, and minister of their sacred rites -- with better fortune than his predecessor. After this formal adoption, he becomes the legal heir, while the offending person is left nameless, childless, and without inheritance."
elegant: "If the exiled person is childless, relatives and authorities select an heir for the vacant household from the state's most virtuous and fortunate families. No house belongs solely to its inhabitant -- all 5,040 are the state's property. Polluted houses must be purified first, then restocked with an adopted heir who continues the family's sacred rites. The criminal is left nameless and portionless."
summary: The Stranger provides for childless criminal households. Since all 5,040 lots are state property, vacated houses are purified and assigned an adopted heir chosen from the most virtuous families. The heir continues the household's sacred functions, while the criminal is erased from family memory.
distilled: Childless criminal households are purified and assigned adopted heirs from virtuous families, since all 5,040 lots belong to the state; the criminal is erased from family record.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage reveals the deeply communal nature of property in the Laws' ideal state. The principle that 'no house of all the 5040 belongs to the inhabitant or to the whole family, but is the public and private property of the state' is one of the Laws' most radical claims about property rights. While less extreme than the Republic's abolition of private property for the guardian class (416d-417b), it establishes that property rights in the Laws are always conditional on the state's needs. The selection of heirs based on both virtue and good fortune (eutychia) is characteristic of the Laws' pragmatic synthesis of moral and practical considerations. The formal adoption ritual -- introduction to the ancestors, naming, and installation as keeper of hearth and rites -- reflects the centrality of the oikos (household) as both an economic unit and a religious institution in Greek life. The ancestral hearth (hestia) was literally the religious center of the home, connecting to the civic hearth of Hestia discussed in 9.7. The erasure of the criminal's name and family line (becoming 'nameless, childless, and portionless') is a form of damnatio memoriae -- total obliteration from social existence.

narrative:
"Now the boundaries of some things do not touch one another, but there is a borderland which comes in between, preventing them from touching. And we were saying that actions done from passion are of this nature, and come in between the voluntary and involuntary.

"If a person be convicted of having inflicted wounds in a passion, in the first place he shall pay twice the amount of the injury, if the wound be curable, or, if incurable, four times the amount of the injury; or if the wound be curable, and at the same time cause great and notable disgrace to the wounded person, he shall pay fourfold.

"And whenever any one in wounding another injures not only the sufferer, but also the city, and makes him incapable of defending his country against the enemy, he, besides the other penalties, shall pay a penalty for the loss which the state has incurred. And the penalty shall be, that in addition to his own times of service, he shall serve on behalf of the disabled person, and shall take his place in war; or, if he refuse, he shall be liable to be convicted by law of refusal to serve."
refashioned:
"Some categories do not share clear boundaries but have a borderland between them. Actions done from passion are precisely this -- they fall in the borderland between voluntary and involuntary.

"If someone is convicted of wounding another in a fit of passion, the penalties shall be as follows: for a curable wound, double the assessed damage. For an incurable wound, four times the damage. If the wound is curable but causes the victim great and conspicuous disgrace, the penalty is fourfold.

"Furthermore, whenever a wound not only harms the individual victim but also damages the state by rendering the person incapable of military service, the attacker must pay an additional penalty for the state's loss. This penalty takes a specific form: in addition to fulfilling his own military obligations, the attacker must serve in the military on behalf of the person he disabled, taking his place in war. If he refuses, he may be prosecuted for refusal to serve."
elegant: "Passionate wounding falls in the borderland between voluntary and involuntary. Penalties: double compensation for curable wounds, quadruple for incurable or disgraceful ones. If the wound renders the victim unfit for military service, the attacker must serve in the military on the victim's behalf, in addition to his own service obligations. Refusal to serve is a prosecutable offense."
summary: The Stranger addresses wounding in passion, which occupies a borderland between voluntary and involuntary. Penalties are proportional: double for curable wounds, quadruple for incurable or disgraceful ones. If the wound renders the victim unfit for military duty, the attacker must personally serve in the victim's place.
distilled: Passionate wounding is penalized by proportional compensation (double for curable, quadruple for incurable or disgraceful wounds), plus mandatory substitute military service if the wound disabled the victim.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The metaphor of 'borderland' (methorios) between the voluntary and involuntary, applied to passionate action, elegantly recapitulates the theoretical discussion from 9.32. The graduated penalty structure -- doubling for curable wounds, quadrupling for incurable ones, and a separate multiplier for wounds causing public disgrace -- represents a sophisticated approach to damages assessment that accounts for both physical and social harm. The military service provision is particularly innovative: by requiring the attacker to serve in the disabled person's place, the law simultaneously compensates the state for its military loss and imposes a personal burden on the attacker that is directly proportional to the harm he caused. This penalty has no close parallel in known Greek law and anticipates the logic of specific performance in modern civil law. The distinction between harm to the individual and harm to the state recognizes that some injuries have public as well as private dimensions -- a concept reflected in modern categories of crimes against the state versus civil torts.

narrative:
"The compensation for the injury, whether to be twofold or threefold or fourfold, shall be fixed by the judges who convict him.

"And if, in like manner, a brother wounds a brother, the parents and kindred of either sex, including the children of cousins, whether on the male or female side, shall meet, and when they have judged the cause, they shall entrust the assessment of damages to the parents, as is natural; and if the estimate be disputed, then the kinsmen on the male side shall make the estimate, or if they cannot, they shall commit the matter to the guardians of the law.

"And when similar charges of wounding are brought by children against their parents, those who are more than sixty years of age, having children of their own, not adopted, shall be required to decide; and if any one is convicted, they shall determine whether he or she ought to die, or suffer some other punishment either greater than death, or, at any rate, not much less. A kinsman of the offender shall not be allowed to judge the cause, not even if he be of the age which is prescribed by the law.

"If a slave in a fit of anger wound a freeman, the owner of the slave shall give him up to the wounded man, who may do as he pleases with him, and if he not give him up he shall himself make good the injury. And if any one says that the slave and the wounded man are conspiring together, let him argue the point, and if he is cast, he shall pay for the wrong three times over, but if he gains his case, the freeman who conspired with the slave shall be liable to an action for kidnapping.

"And if any one unintentionally wounds another he shall simply pay for the harm, for no legislator is able to control chance. In such a case the judges shall be the same as those who are appointed in the case of children suing their parents; and they shall estimate the amount of the injury."
refashioned:
"The exact compensation -- whether double, triple, or quadruple -- shall be determined by the judges who hear the case.

"When a brother wounds a brother, the parents and relatives of both sides, including second cousins on both the male and female line, shall assemble and judge the matter. They shall naturally delegate the assessment of damages to the parents. If the parents' estimate is disputed, the paternal relatives shall decide; if they cannot resolve it, the matter goes to the guardians of the law.

"When children bring wounding charges against their parents, the judges must be citizens over sixty who have biological children of their own (not adopted). If a parent is convicted, these judges shall determine whether the penalty should be death or some other punishment of comparable severity -- not much less than death. No kinsman of the accused may serve as judge, regardless of his age or qualifications.

"If a slave wounds a free person in anger, the slave's owner must surrender the slave to the wounded person, who may do whatever he wishes with him. If the owner refuses, he must personally compensate for the injury. If someone claims the slave and the wounded person staged the incident in collusion, the claim may be argued in court. If the claim fails, the accuser pays triple damages. If it succeeds, the free person who conspired with the slave faces prosecution for kidnapping.

"If someone wounds another unintentionally, he simply pays for the harm caused, since no legislator can control chance. The judges in such cases shall be the same as those who decide cases of children suing parents, and they shall assess the injury."
elegant: "Judges fix the exact compensation. Family wounding cases (brother on brother) are judged by assembled relatives, with parents assessing damages. When children accuse parents of wounding, judges must be citizens over sixty with their own children; penalties range up to death. No relative of the accused may judge. Slaves who wound free persons are surrendered to the victim. Unintentional wounding requires simple compensation, since no law can control chance."
summary: The Stranger details wounding provisions for specific relationships: family wounds are judged by assembled relatives with parents assessing damages; children suing parents require independent elderly judges with severe penalties possible; slave assaults on free persons result in surrender of the slave; unintentional wounding requires only compensation for the harm.
distilled: Family wounding cases are judged by relatives, children's charges against parents require independent elderly judges with penalties up to death, and unintentional wounding requires only compensation since law cannot control chance.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The family adjudication system for sibling wounding reflects the Greek tradition of handling intra-family disputes within the family unit rather than through public courts -- the oikos as self-governing legal entity. The requirement that judges of parental wounding charges must be over sixty with their own biological children ensures both life experience and personal understanding of the parent-child relationship. The prohibition against kinsmen serving as judges in these cases establishes a conflict-of-interest principle. The slave-and-freeman collusion provision addresses a practical concern: a free person might arrange to be 'wounded' by a slave to obtain the slave as property. The triple penalty for false accusations of collusion deters abuse of this defense. The final principle -- 'no legislator is able to control chance' -- echoes the acknowledgment from 9.49 that law can only address generalities. Compare Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics 3.1 (1110a-1111b) on the distinction between voluntary and involuntary action, where chance (tuche) features as a category beyond moral assessment.

narrative:
"All the preceding injuries and every kind of assault are deeds of violence; and every man, woman, or child ought to consider that the elder has the precedence of the younger in honour, both among the Gods and also among men who would live in security and happiness. Wherefore it is a foul thing and hateful to the Gods to see an elder man assaulted by a younger in the city; and it is reasonable that a young man when struck by an elder should lightly endure his anger, laying up in store for himself a like honour when he is old.

"Let this be the law: Every one shall reverence his elder in word and deed; he shall respect any one who is twenty years older than himself, whether male or female, regarding him or her as his father or mother; and he shall abstain from laying hands on any one who is of an age to have been his father or his mother, out of reverence to the Gods who preside over birth; similarly he shall keep his hands from a stranger, whether he be an old inhabitant or newly arrived; he shall not venture to correct such an one by blows, either as the aggressor or in self-defence.

"If he thinks that some stranger has struck him out of wantonness or insolence, and ought to be punished, he shall take him to the wardens of the city, but let him not strike him, that the stranger may be kept far away from the possibility of lifting up his hand against a citizen, and let the wardens of the city take the offender and examine him, not forgetting their duty to the God of Strangers, and in case the stranger appears to have struck the citizen unjustly, let them inflict upon him as many blows with the scourge as he has himself inflicted, and quell his presumption."
refashioned:
"All the injuries discussed so far are acts of violence. Every person -- man, woman, and child -- must understand that elders take precedence over the younger in honor, both among the gods and among people who wish to live securely and happily. It is therefore disgraceful and hateful to the gods when a younger person assaults an elder in the city. When a young person is struck by an elder, it is reasonable for the young person to endure the blow patiently, storing up the same deference for himself when he reaches old age.

"Let the law be this: Everyone shall show reverence to their elders in word and deed. Anyone twenty or more years older shall be treated as a father or mother, regardless of sex. No one shall lay hands on anyone old enough to have been his parent, out of reverence for the gods who preside over birth. The same protection extends to strangers, whether long-established or newly arrived; no one shall presume to correct a stranger with blows, either as aggressor or in self-defense.

"If a citizen believes a stranger has struck him out of wantonness or insolence, he shall bring the stranger before the city wardens rather than striking back -- this prevents any situation where a stranger might raise his hand against a citizen. The wardens shall examine the case, mindful of their obligation to Zeus, the God of Strangers. If the stranger is found to have struck the citizen unjustly, the wardens shall scourge him with exactly as many blows as he inflicted, suppressing his presumption."
elegant: "Elders hold precedence in honor among both gods and humans. Assaulting an elder is foul; a young person struck by an elder should endure it patiently. The law: revere anyone twenty or more years older as a parent. Never strike anyone old enough to be your parent, or any stranger. If a stranger strikes you, bring him to the wardens rather than retaliating. The wardens, honoring the God of Strangers, shall scourge him with equal blows if he struck unjustly."
summary: The Stranger establishes laws protecting elders and strangers from assault. Anyone twenty or more years older must be treated as a parent. Young people must endure blows from elders patiently. Strangers who assault citizens are examined by wardens and scourged; citizens must not retaliate directly.
distilled: Elders and strangers receive special legal protection from assault: anyone twenty years older must be treated as a parent, and disputes with strangers must be referred to city wardens rather than resolved by personal retaliation.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The principle of elder reverence is deeply rooted in Greek culture and appears prominently in both Spartan custom (where young men stood for elders and deferred to their authority) and Athenian social norms. The specific threshold of twenty years -- treating anyone twenty years older as equivalent to a parent -- provides a precise, enforceable rule. The protection of strangers reflects the Greek institution of xenia (guest-friendship) and the religious importance of Zeus Xenios (Zeus as protector of strangers), explicitly mentioned here. The requirement to bring complaints to wardens rather than retaliating directly serves multiple purposes: it prevents escalation, maintains public order, and ensures that the stranger receives fair examination rather than mob justice. The scourging penalty -- exactly matching the number of blows inflicted -- applies the law of retaliation (lex talionis) in its most literal form. Compare Odyssey 14.56-58, where even the swineherd Eumaeus invokes Zeus Xenios to justify hospitality to the disguised Odysseus.

narrative:
